## Greg

Greg’s legs leaped off the pegs \- the back wheel had locked under heavy braking. *So much for ABS* he’d think later. The chaos of cars screeching to a stop continued as he caught the weight of the bike with his right booted foot. 

Car horns and distant yelling. Half a dozen commuters and a truck had reacted suddenly to the burst flow of morning traffic on busy Anzac Parade. Greg stood the bike up and checked his extremities. No obvious pain or torn clothing. The bike had stalled. He rocked it back and forth and moved the transmission from 4th gear to neutral, conscious of his rapid heartbeat and vague pain down the back of his calves. He opened his helmet and looked around.

Drivers were hanging their heads out open windows and some were getting out of their cars. The heavy traffic behind the cluster of scattered vehicles had stopped. Greg put the bike on the side stand and started removing his gloves.

“What are you doing?” yelled the hi-vis driver of a small delivery van. His target was the driver of a silver hatchback at the front of the tangled emergency stop. As Greg approached, he saw the destroyed front tyre of the silver hatchback, the grey face of the grand-fatherly driver who appeared to have been making a radical left turn from the middle lane when the flow of traffic tipped into radical randomness.

The truck driver continued to yell at the terrified driver of the Silver Hatchback. Other voices rose. “Hey\!\! Back off man”. “Calm down fella…”. Greg was starting to unbuckle his helmet. His pulse was returning to normal. He assessed the scene. Silver Hatchback had veered left very suddenly \- probably when a near flat steering tyre’s sidewall peeled in two. Traffic was hit by a spell of Randomness and driver reactions became pure embodied self defence. 

Silver Hatchback and the old Toyota in the adjacent left lane were entangled with crushed panels. The old Toyota driver had exited through their passenger door. Greg did a slow 360\. Other than shock and mood aberrations, nobody appeared hurt. 

Greg looked behind his parked bike. A young face stared back at him. “Thanks for not running into me,” Greg said. Smiles exchanged.

Silver Hatchback would need a tow. The cluster of people around the accident were sending messages and making calls. Greg made a decision. He pushed the bike back and forth, lock to lock and climbed on, hitting the starter. Moving into a narrow space, he headed towards the footpath. The driver to his left backed up a little, making the space more usable. Greg nodded to his considerate fellow commuter, and jumped the curb, returning to the road in front of Silver Hatchback, stopping nearby for a red light.

“HEY\!” Yelled visy-vest. The traffic light turned green. “Who do you think you are?” Greg heard as he accelerated away. *Good question* he said to himself.

## Uncle Ern

“Watch your fingers Mike,” said Uncle Ern. Mike was trying to slip a bloodworm onto his fishhook. The process was looking somewhat shaky.

“Here, let me try Mike”.

“I can DO it\!” Mike said, feeling red in the face. 

Progress. The centre of the worm was threading its way past the barb and onto the shank of the fishhook. Mike stabbed his fingers a couple of times, but persisted. He presented the baited hook to his uncle. “Nice job Mike”.

Ernest Bennington found the purchase to stand on the canal’s muddy bank, ready to assist if Mike had trouble with his hand line. Getting up from a squatted position had been a struggle for the last 20 of Ern’s 76 years on planet earth. He held his breath through the autonomic groan that Ern would emit a dozen times per day.  

Mike’s first couple of casting efforts didn’t make the deeper section of the waterway by which they stood. Rather than repeating a lecture, Ern pulled his own line in, preparing for another cast. As he spun the sinker he said to Mike “is this centripetal or centrifugal force, letting me spin the sinker here Mike?”. Ern let the sinker go at the bottom of its arc, dropping it in the middle of the canal. 

“What’s the difference?” said Mike.

“Just your point of view,” said Ern. “Centrifugal is taking the perspective of the sinker \- centripetal is more the way your finger is experiencing the force”. Mike spun and cast again. Much better this time. They sat, Ern on the esky and Mike on a tree root.

“So, why does that matter?” asked Mike.

“Well, I wanted you to try a new way of thinking about the forces you’re dealing with in casting a hand line.  A different perspective can be like a reset of a situation. Since I’d already shown you a way to cast a couple of times, I thought it was time for a different perspective.”

Mike smiled. Ern was a Tardis Brain if ever there was one. “Tardis Brain” was an Uncle Ern expression too. Many of Ern’s expressions formed a library of metaphors that set his speech aside from the bland, normal world. *I respect the old fossil, Mike thought*. Sixty years was just the right age gap in age to avoid the resentment teenagers often felt for their parents' generation.

“So, tell me some more stuff about centrifugal force Uncle Ern”

Ern was quiet for a moment while checking the movements on his line for a fish. 

## 

##  Aunty

Adrian parked his old banger on Aunty’s lawn. The engine ran on for a few seconds. Not a good sign. There’s a build up of carbon at the top of the engine. This will be hot enough to cause fuel to ignite sporadically. The sound of an engine running on will be a sign of something wrong, even to the least automotively experienced.

“Hello Aunty,” Adrian called from the front step. 

“Come in Adrian\! I just made a cup of tea”. Adrian smiled. He often wondered how many packs of tea leaves Aunty went through. She was a central figure in Albion Park for the Maori community. There was always someone dropping for a cuppa. 

*You really gotta wonder,* Adrian thought. Many of his extended family moved here to the south coast of NSW over the last 20 years. Austerity in NZ meant hard times. The open Australia New Zealand immigration policy led to many Kiwi’s trying their  luck in Oz. The results were a mix bag at best. Adrian’s family were doing it pretty tough here and then.

“What’s news Adrian?”. Aunty hugged him and directed him to a chair at her kitchen table. The teapot wore a woollen cosy that had its own comforting aroma. Aunty often pulled the tea cosy on as a hat during tea ceremonies in her kitchen. “Take my word” she’d say. “Anyone left in a room with a tea cosy will try it on as a hat. If they don’t \- there’s something wrong going on there”.

“Got some news Aunty.” Said Adrian. “Remember Greg Bennington, the fella I worked with in Sydney?” 

“Yeah, I do,” said Aunty. “A  solid fella as I recall. For a honkey, that is..”

Adrian laughed. “Yep, he’s a decent guy. Always did the right thing by me. Anyway, he’s working for a company that’s doing software development and they’re hiring. I should say Greg is hiring. I think he’s the boss at this place. He’s offered me a job. A good one by the sounds”

“Well that’s great Adrian. Will you need to go to the city for this? You know, everyone's zooming and working from home these days. We’d miss you if you take off to the city”

“Yes Aunty, I know. I’m gonna rent a room in Coogee. All the work’s around the city and east. I can come back on weekends”

“Mmm. Yeah, you might come and visit…. But your life will be up in Sydney. You can’t be in two places at once.” said Aunty. She smiled.

“Look love, I know you need to go ahead with your life. It’s been great having you around since Annie died. You’re still young enough to make a life with a family of your own. Don’t mess about too long\!”

Adrian smiled at Aunty Tia. “I don’t know if I’m up for that kind of thing Aunty. You know me. I get bored easily. Besides, settling down means picking a country. I like it here, but I often get this powerful feeling that I need to be Akora before I put down too many roots”. 

Aunty put her hand on his. “I know darl. I get that too. I’ve had half a lifetime here, but it doesn’t feel like home. I’ll stick with it, but I want to go home… Even if I die here, I want my body to go home.”

“Silly in some ways” she said. “But that’s how I see it. How we see it. How the Kouri see it. The land *owns* us. The world’s got it all arse backwards. Owning land…”

“I get it Aunty Tia. Look, the world is waking up, well, younger people are. So much of the WEIRD world is about Ownership, especially land and where people live. I could never get organised to buy a place… but I think I might have done myself a favour. “

Aunty laughed. “The only thing you own is your body. Anything else you try and own, ends up owning you”. 

“RIght Aunty. I’m telling you, there’s people along the edges of this world here, they get that. They’re getting clues from buddhists. People write about toxic Consumption and kids want to get into Land Care. I told you about the Young Agrarians?”

“Yes, you did,” said Aunty Tia. “In the green patch of Athabasca. They sound like great kids. Bringing science to land care. Do you wonder why that kind of movement didn’t kick into life in New Zealand 20 years ago? 40 years ago?”

Adrian looked calm, masking a dubious reaction to what Aunty was about to say. 

“The Pākehā world we were living in 40 years ago was like all the colonies… all the offspring of industry. Whether we’re talking gunpowder, steam or tractors, all the power came from ancient sunlight. The kids in Alberta have had a life rich in fossil energy. That’s what woke them up to Land Care…. But they never would have had the resources to Wake Up without a few generations of abundant life.. If it’s fair to call poutine and Moulson’s ‘abundant’” she smiled.

They took a moment to look at each other. 

Aunty had a downwise smile as she said. “Don’t worry Love, my memory’s not shot yet. I was your age once. I remember ideas from elders that seemed out of date to me.”

“I’ll tell you the best bit of anything I’ve learned as I’ve grown older. I’ve been wrong about nearly everything. The more sure I was, the more life set about showing me, there is no Sure.”

“Will you do me a favour before you go?” said Tia. “Spend some time with Sparkles before you go. She’s not going very well at the moment. Fighting with her Dad and brothers… they’re all grieving and it’s making her feel isolated as the only girl.”

“Yeah, of course,” said Adrian. “I can go over to their place now if you like”

“She’s not there at the moment,” said Tia. “I talked to Chris this morning. There was a bit of a row and some shouting going on this morning. Sparks ran off into the bush. She’s been gone for hours”

“I’ll find her Aunty. Thanks for the cuppa. I’ll let you know what happens”. Greg hugged Tia’s shoulders as she stared into her tea leaves.

## Sparkes

Heeni slowed down to a walk and wandered down the path. Her face burned under the tears. There was an ache in her belly \- the same one that was keeping her awake at night lately.  She felt weak through her lower back, as if the belly pain went right through her spine.

She stumbled over a tree root and fell face first onto the leaf littered track. *Nice work Sparkle* she thought. The aches and the angst seemed to fall even lower. Then into the ground. She rolled on her side and looked at her hands. A few scratches. Slowly, she climbed to her feet and shook herself, looking at her hands. *How did my hands get in front of my face just now, in the second I tripped?* The trees spoke the breeze around her. She stared into the wave passing through leaves overhead. The wind dropped to nothing. Silence.

With some surprise, she realised she’d stopped crying and was breathing steadily. She heard water running in the distance and instantly knew where she was going.

Even though the fall had knocked her intense emotions off to the side, she was still hot and aching in many places. The running water was a waterfall and swimming hole nearby. For whatever reason, the place was always empty when she visited there. The idea of a shower under the waterfall occupied the front of mind.

Her mind provided some music… Lisa Gerrard’s track Sacrifice. The expanse of Lisa’s voice ran through her emotions \- uplifting, sad and serious. Grandeur. 

She approached the swimming hole from higher ground, using the lantana for a foothold, grateful she hadn’t run off barefoot. 

She slipped off her boots and stepped into the water, jeans and all. She walked around the edges of the pool to the waterfall. Oblivious to the shock of cold, she stood face first into the flow and let out a scream at the world. 

A minute later, the shock of cold water passed. Heeni stepped into the waterhole and submerged slowly, holding her breath on the stoney creek bed. Her pulse slowed in the ear canals filled with water, noticing the flow of the creek. *Three minutes,* she thought. *Todd Sampson learned to hold his breath for three minutes in order to repeat the Houdini underwater escape.*  She felt her body rebelling against the held breath, surfacing and taking a deep breath.

A tree frog stared at her from the rocks on the pool's edge. “Hello little guy\!” she said. She paddled over to the frog, keeping eye contact. “Watcha doin down here Froggy?” The frog said nothing through pursed lips. 

Heeni rested her chin on folded arms staring into the frog's left eye, wondering about the visual images a frog might experience. “Who cares what it’s Like to be a bat?” she said. “I’d like to know what it’s like to be a frog, looking all sideways and all”. She lay her left hand next to Froggy, face down.

Heeni stayed very still for what seemed an age. The frog slowly crawled onto the back of her hand. Show glowed and smiled \- and noticed that the belly pain, the heart ache and even the cold of the water, receded.  She moved her face nose to nose with the tiny life form and let gratitude flow through herself.

Finally the chilled water around her encouraged her to break the spell. “I’ve got to get some sunlight, Froggy. You coming?”. 

She walked through the creek bed and climbed onto a sunlit sandstone flet, sitting down, her back resting on a tree trunk. Froggy remained suction-cupped to her hand. She moved her hand and passenger into the shade. “There you go Bro\! Dappled sunlight. Are you a sister or a mister?”. Froggy said nothing, neck a slow moving set of bellows.

Heeni shut her eyes and enjoyed the sunshine. She could feel the little life form on her hand. Was that a heartbeat or breathing? It seemed to be slowing.

She slept.

## WhoComm

Greg removed his helmet and looked at what he’d just done. His bike was on the centre stand, wedged between two others. The all-day motorbike parks were hard to find, even at 8am.

He retrieved his right boot from the tangle of exhaust pipes and footpegs. He’d had to walk the bike into the narrow space, from the front. Leave it leaning on a neighbouring bike and approach the task of getting on the stand from the kerb side. A brief feeling of success over lifting his bike onto the stand was replaced by heat and pain where his booted foot was trapped between the two parked motorcycles.

There were a few smiles from passers by as he Houdinied himself from the foot trap. His retrieved boot smelled of cooked leather. 

He dug out his phone to check messages and the time. A pack of cigarette papers came out of his pocket and he caught it as it fell towards the concrete. There was time for a quick smoke. He rolled up a racehorse. Scrolled through some emails.

He’d pushed a few tweaks to the server-side of the WhoComm platform overnight. They were demonstrating to the Premier's Department today. The registries of Births, Deaths and Marriages were crawling through Federal system requirements and trying to hold their heads up from the Roads and Maritime Service, who controlled drivers licences.

WhoComm is more than a gig for Greg. His partners and bosses found the money and the legal support to pursue his Idea. 

“People *want* a solid, government trusted Identity… sometimes”. He’d opened with this when he met Pak-Nim at his friend Song’s 21st birthday party.

They’d gone on to form WhoComm, based in Sydney and Seoul. Greg climbed the stairs to the third floor office and walked into a loud conversation.

“Just forget it man\!”. Eric was standing up and waving his arms at the blank faces around the table. “Anytime you decentralise the secrets, the potential for trust drops through the floor. Whoever you are talking to is living in Fairyland”

Eric’s pale face was flushed with high temperament.  The three faces looking back at him were Mae, Vicki and Roy, the new folks at WhoComm. Mae was staring at her hands, Vicki looking at Greg for support. Roy stared straight back at Eric, his face taught. 

“Well, here’s a cheery bunch of people,” said Greg. “Isn't it a bit early for the loss of cool folks?”

“Yeah, sorry Greg” said Eric, losing the flush in his cheeks. “We’ve.. I..”. He took a breath and continued. “We've been talking about some mathematical and cryptographic fantasies \- and this protocol is hard enough without throwing Whitfield Diffie out the window.”

Vicki spoke up. “Greg, you told us about the limitations we live with from how Certificate Authorities are licensed and supported by governments and that often goes ‘the way of the pear’ is how you described it.”

“That’d be me,” replied Greg. “Eric and I always argue about this. All of our modern cryptography depends on governments playing by some rules… not to mention Three Letter Agencies being open about policies and proofs. That isn’t what we’re seeing and experiencing.”

“And there’s been nothing that’s emerged from decades of high grade, high volume cryptography that shows us anything novel \- prime numbers and protection of secrets, that’s what it all swings on”.

“No strong crypto, no strong Identity” Roy quoted an axiom often heard around the office. “But also, no strong Identity, no strong secrets. No strong secrets, no strong crypto”

“Ah Discordia\!” said Mae and Eric in unison.

Laughter. A little relief from the tension in the room.

“So, it’s been an interesting morning already,” said Greg. I came within a few centimetres of a bike accident this morning. Some random stranger kept me whole by hanging back a bit in traffic. I’d been hoping for some flow-on effect of luck and life at work today. “

“So, what gives folks? We’ve all been here before a dozen times. We’re making trade-offs in an imperfect world..”

“The Universe is perfect. We will improve it”. This time, Roy was quoting Pak-Nim. Eric rolled his eyes.

“Beer Tax\!” Mae had looked up from her hands. She was pointing at Eric and smiling. 

“Ok, ok, ok. My shout at the pub tonight”. Eric’s demeanour had softened. He’d broken their office code with his disdain. Pak-Nim didn’t have a lot of cultural rules, but he’d made Eye Rolling a beer taxable transgression.

“Can you read this please?” Roy handed Greg a dozen page document, stapled. 

“Wow, paper\!” said Greg, looking at the front page. “Secrets with Symmetry” he read aloud, quietly reading the first paragraph. “Where did this come from?”

Silence. Mae and Vicki were downcast, but Roy was looking directly into Greg’s eyes.

“Nobody feels like answering that one,” said Eric. “Which is largely why I’m so sceptical about what it says”

“Well, this is pretty weird” said Greg after a longer pause in conversation. “So, not you Roy \- this didn’t come from you, or to you. And you’re not prepared to, shall we say, Reveal?”

Roy nodded. 

“All right. I’ve got some code to review but I’ll read this as soon as I get on top of it” said Greg. He handed the document back to Roy, who kept his hands on the desk. “Can’t someone send me a copy electronically?”

“We don’t have an electronic copy. Not yet,” said Mae. 

“Not yet?” Greg sounded incredulous. He resisted the temptation to let his eyes roll and finally said “Old Kennebunkport” 

“Kinderhook” said Eric.

The three youthful faces at the table showed variations on Confused. 

“Is there an Etymologist in the House?”. It was Eric in a Python-esq tone. More blank stares. “Relax guys, it’s an In joke, you had to be there.” 

Everyone wandered off to their day.

## A good day for travelling 

“Sparkles\! Sparky\! Sparkes\!” Adrian had a fair idea where Heeni would have run off to. Adrian had known her from the day she was born. He’d been amazed to feel how well Heeni senses what was going on for him. A random text to say, “hey, how are you doing?” had often arrived from her at just the right time.

Adrian Cooeed, called her name, whistled with two and four fingers. He stood still and listened. He heard the waterfall and he… knew.

“Cuzz. Hey Cuss\!” Adrian called from the far side of the waterhole. Heeni was sound asleep when he found her. She stirred. Blinked. Consciousness emerging from sleep. Adrian had a moment of wonderment, pondering sleep and living things.

Heeni turned her head a little and saw Adrian. She smiled and sat up.

“You were having a nice sleep in the sun,” said Adrian, smiling back. 

Heeni’s clothes were nearly dry. She felt pleasantly warm and sleepy. She looked at her hands. “I found a little green friend,” she said in a dream-ridden tone. 

“So you did,” said Adrian. “I’m not sure who I feel about my Cuzz getting all up close and personal with Frogs. Soon you’ll be kissing him and having to deal with some poncey Prince”

Heeni laughed. “I think he’s a she. I’m not sure”. She kissed the sleeping frog. No royal ponces appeared. “I’m hungry… I must have been asleep for a long time.”

“Common Cuzz. Let’s go and eat. Pub hamburger?”

“Sounds great,” said Heeni. “Do they let Frogs in the pub?”. She stood and stretched. The frog was awake now. She held her hand near a branch. The frog climbed off her hand. “Thank’s Froggy. I needed that. Magic Sleep Inducing Froggy. I hope you’re here when I come back next time”. 

They found the trail and walked together. Adrian decided not to ask about today’s events. It didn’t seem like a great time to share his news about a job in Sydney either.   
“My car’s at the lookout.” Greg said. “No tourists today. They’re missing out on this shiny blue winter's day.”

Heeni sang “Sunday Drivers a cruising round…. Wish they’d all go back to town.” 

Adriand “What do they expect to find”

“Sure as hell and peace of mind…” they sang in unison.

They carried on with their Richard Clapton tribute as they trudged uphill to Adrians’s old Camry, sitting lonely in its poo-brown and dusty glory. Creaky door hinges admitted them to the atmosphere of cooking apple cores, engine oil and cigarettes. Heeni fiddled with the radio as they rumbled down Macquarie Pass towards the coast.

Adrian jumped on the brakes and took a right turn. “Sorry, I changed my mind. Jambaroo Pub should be open and not too busy today”.  

Heeni was having no joy finding music on the radio. Adrian reached under the dash and wobbled the aerial cable. Indie rock bloomed through the single functional back speaker.

“It’s a method Actor” said Adrian. “The car is a method actor. “

Heeni punched his shoulder. “Car actor. Character. I get it. It’s just not funny”.

“A good pun gets a punch \- go on, admit it, you think I’m punny.” Adrian was smiling.

“You just turned my pun punch into a play on words… will you stop it\!\!” Heeni was holding  another shoulder punch indecisively. She was smiling too, despite her best attempts to fake upset.

“I’ve groan used to you” she said. Adrian now tried a shoulder punch back at her.  

A new song came on the radio, the style very different, a little mournful. “Hey, I love this song...Turn it up. Please,” said Adrian, taking the wheel in both hands to be a bit more cautious with his driving.

“Who the hell is this?” asked Heeni.  
“Just have a listen”  asked Adrian in a steady voice.

Heeni relented and listened. Adrian sang along. He appeared to know this song by heart.

They were pulling up at the pub as the song ended. Heeni looked at his face. Yes, his eyes were teary. “What is that song? Why does it pull at my heart and make me happy and sad all at once?” Heeni asked.

Adrian pulled on the handbrake and looked at her. “There are just some songs… that’s what Aunty says. They have this ability to just Turn Up at the right time.”

“Yeah, I know about pattern recognition,” he continued. “It’s ‘just a coincidence’ they say. So What, I say. What’s ‘just’ a coincidence? That’s Chris Rea telling a story about life and death and how we deal with it. A little girl asking her grandad to Tell her there’s a Heaven. She needs someone to tell her… reinforce the idea. Tell her that it’s So.”

Heeni stared at him, emotions rising in her eyes. “Aunty,” she said. “I need to tell her I’m ok”. 

Adrian held out his phone to her, rose out of the car and went to find a seat in the winter sunshine.

\-- 

Adrian pushed back his plate and wiped his lips. He had a sip of his beer and watched Heeni finishing off her meal, sopping up the juice with the remainder of her hamburger bun. “I’m not a vegan by any stretch,” she said. “Could I get another beer?” she asked.

“Stay there \- I’ve got it”. Adrian wandered into the bar, had a quick look at his messages. A thank you message from Aunty. He smiled and carried the beers back to their table.

“Thanks so much Cuzz,” she said. “I needed a friend today, and you just Turned Up.” She looked at Adrian’s upside down smile. “You knew\!” she said. “Did Aunty send you…”

She looked down. A little of the magic ran out of her afternoon; disappointing. 

“Of course she told me Cuzz. But she didn’t chase me down and ask me to find you. I was over at hers.. Over at hers to tell her some news”.  
Heeni sat up and waited to hear the rest of the story.

“Look, don’t feel bad because people love you and care about you,” said Adrian. “Just like Aunty, I want to be there for you, but not all over you. We all know what an independent soul you are.”

Adrian held out hands with crossed arms. She responded in kind and they held each other's hands across the table.

Heeni spoke after a few moments of silence. “So, why am I feeling… Why does it seem like, just when you’ve been Here for me and told me you are going to be There for me… that you’re about to leave me?”

Adrian held her gaze. The food and beer and sunshine and company had frog sleep had all done their job, but this was still a difficult conversation. “I’ve got a job offer in Sydney. A good one. I’ve said yes to it”

Heeni drew a sobbing breath. “Take me with you please? I just can’t deal with mum’s death and Dad’s moods..and school and being the family cook and…” She squeezed Adrian’s hands. They sat in silence till she withdrew her hands to wipe her eyes.

“When are you going? How soon? Where will you be living? Will you come back on weekends?” Heeni was crying openly.

Adrian sat back and mentally chewed on his response. He went with the plain truth. “I start next week… and yes, I can come back from time to time. I’m sharing a flat in Coogee and working in town. “

“And you can come and be my housemate, of course. Don’t you think you should finish school first?”

## Blank face

Greg stood near Roy’s desk and looked at him. Sort of looked at him. He was looking nowhere. And everywhere.

“That was my first reaction too,” said Roy. “I was like ‘no way’. Then ‘way\!’. Then no WAY\! And then, Oh whhhhaaaaaaatt?” 

“I need a smoke”. Greg walked off with his thoughts stop/starting and no idea where he was going.

Down on the street, he thought about the touch-parked motorbike and had a look. One of his neighbouring bikes had left, having folded back Greg’s side mirror and footpegs. Greg took the opportunity to shuffle the bike into a more even space. Then he reached into his pocket for his tobacco.

“I leave home without my head sometimes, but I never forget my tobacco”. Uncle Ern had said that more than once. “Nicotine addiction is very hard to explain,” said Ern. Some people just bind to the chemicals in a knot that doesn’t want to come undone”

Greg wondered if he had left the building without his head. Tobacco addiction runs in families and Greg had a similar dose to his uncle. As he contemplated this, Mae emerged from the building and handed him his tobacco pouch.

“Thanks. I thought you didn’t like me smoking” said Greg.

“I don't,” said Mae. “My granddad had emphysema. I’m never gonna be a fan.”

“And your Dad died in a motorbike accident. It can’t be easy for you to see how I live my life” said Greg. 

“Your life, your choices \- I don’t judge,” said Mae. “But, you’re right, there are some subconscious things going on there. I love this job. And WhoComm should be called GregComm really”. Mae smiled. 

“Thanks anyway,” said Greg, lighting up. *I’m back, he* thought. *Wherever I just was, I’m back.*  
“What a headfuck that Secrets with Symmetry thing is\!” Greg looked at Mae. “What?” he said. “I presume you read it. Do you understand it?”

“I don’t understand it. Not yet anyway,” she said. “It was me. I brought the paper into our office.”

Greg waited for more.

“The person that gave me that document asked me to make a few promises. Don’t share it with anyone except the techos at WhoComm. Don’t copy, OCR, transcribe… and if I do all that, we’ll get the first mover advantage”.

“So, you can’t or won’t say who gave it to you?” asked Greg.

“I don’t know who gave it to me. It’s hard to explain. When you read it again, have a look at how it’s laid out. Every description and use case and proof is split across two pages. I received all the odd numbered pages in my letterbox, in a plain envelope”

“Ok,” said Greg. “I didn’t notice that, but that might be one of the many reasons it’s so hard to take in. What about the even numbered pages?”

“I received this Twitter DM.” Mae held her phone out to Greg. He read the note, which appeared to be from Mae’s gym, letting her know that she’d left her backpack in the locker room.

“It looked kosher at first glance. I don’t put my name or details on labels… just my twitter handle. I didn’t look at the Twitter ID, just the friendly name. Have a closer look” said Mae, offering her phone again.

**Enmore Yoga Space**   
@man

“@man?” said Greg. That doesn’t look real. So you got this message and..”

“I went to the gym on my way home. They had a backpack  in the lost and found. It wasn’t mine. But it had a plain white envelope in it, with my name on it. Not my Twitter handle \- my name. I was a bit freaked out, but I took the envelope and went home”

“The other envelope, the odd pages, was in my letterbox. I opened both of them, arranged the pages. There are no numbers on any pages, but that was easy enough to do. I read the whole thing.”

Greg was too enthralled to have doubts. “Go on \- what next?” he asked.

“Well, I was a bit freaked out. I went over to my brother’s place for dinner and tried not to think about it… thought I’d bring the doc into work the next day, today I mean. When I got home from dinner, I jumped online for a while. I received this email”. She held out her phone again, a screenshot.

Thanks for helping out Mae,

It’s time we took our digital identity back from the powers that be. Our digital identity is one of the strongest expressions of our human identity in these times.

We will be opening up the test sites for verification in a few days. 

Please treat our paper as confidential to you and WhoComm. It’s no big deal to us if the paper leaks… but it might be a big deal to you guys, as you will see. 

Project Atman.

“Huh? Double huh?” said Greg. “Project ‘soul’?”

“It’s a reference to Ken Wilber, the Integral guy,” Mae said. ”One of his books is called The Atman Project. But check this out,” she made a few screen gestures and offered her phone one more time.

Greg read through the SMTP header. Mae had highlighted one of the domain key signatures  
DKIM-Signature: v=1; a=rsa-sha256; q=dns/txt; c=relaxed/simple; s=m2kefu7dfatrgytzx6kgkt4lkfjg7qrm; d=man;

“Top level domain of ‘man’. This looks like bullshit” said Greg. “Twitter handle of @man and top level DNS of ‘man’. That can’t be right” said Greg. 

“I know, I know,” said Mae. If you look now, there is no Twitter handle of @man. Of course, there’s no top level DNS name called ‘man’ either. But Greg.. Last night, while I’m trying to figure out this Project Atman email, I did a namespace lookup. Last night, there *was* an email domain called ‘.man’ \- a top level DNS record, a type of mail exchanger. It was there. I swear it was there. It’s not there now.”

Greg asked “Did you get the IPs?”

“No \- I was spinning out. It was a bullshit IP too I think… class A,  11 dot something something. I called Roy. He came over. I was afraid to be alone. I had to tell somebody. By the time Roy turned up, the domain name ‘man’ had vanished, like it was never there.”

“Fair enough,'' said Greg. “I’m freaking out and I wasn’t stuck in the weirdness like you.”

“Well, one thing might make a bit of sense,” Greg said. “There was a reserved Class A of 11 that was never in use. A couple of years ago, these routes to network address 11 suddenly came into use. There was never a great explanation of why this happened and who was behind it. I’m inclined to think it was the US military who exposed the routes.”

“But the fake Twitter account and transient mail domain… that’s State Actor stuff… it’s more than State Actor stuff.” Greg was rolling another cigarette.

“Well, I’m more conflicted than scared,” said Mae. “Maybe a Project Atman, a project soul is benign or even super white hat. It’s the email sender address that’s been making me smile though.”

Greg looked a little lost. “The email sender  is ‘the@man’. The Atman. Get it? Ringing a bell yet?”. 

Mae was smiling from ear to ear. “I can see it now. When we want to talk to the@man, we do a Commissioner Gordon. Except our signal is a giant @ symbol, not a Bat.”

## Sydney Calling

Ern grunted at the ‘interesting’ timing. He’d been gutting and scaling the flathead and bream, hands covered in slimy fish inners and outers as his phone started ringing. *I’ll miss the call by the time I get clean hands,* he thought as he ran water over his hands.

He towelled off his hands and dug for the phone in his overalls hip pocket. It was still ringing. 

“Hello Greg\! I’m amazed I managed to pick up this call \- I was busy cleaning some fish” sad Ern.

“Good to hear your voice Uncle,” said Greg. “I wish this was purely a social call”.

“Hang on” said Ern, getting his hands properly dry and walking out of the garage towards the giant tree behind his house, sitting down on a milk crate. He broke out tobacco and his pipe and wedged the phone on his shoulder as he cleaned and repacked the pipe. “Sorry mate, if we’re up for a chat…”

“Way ahead of you Uncle” Greg said, lighting up. “There’s some weird stuff going on here Uncle Ern. I’m a bit unsure about where to go with it.” Greg layed out some of the story of the last couple of days.”

“Symmetric Secrets” Ern mused. “Well, that doesn’t sound like news from a history perspective. But, you’re saying there are no keys to manage, ephemeral or otherwise? “Hmph” Ern sat back against the tree and lit his pipe. “Well, I don’t see how that’s possible, but that doesn’t mean that much by itself. Ern did a little ‘belly talk’ as he called the descending hum he emitted when thinking. 

“Here’s the thing, Uncle Ern,” said Greg. “There is some seriously weird stuff going on in terms of how we were contacted. This could easily be a scam… and just as easily an unfriendly party, even a competitor messing with us.” Greg went quiet.

“Well, it’s certainly entertaining. I’d imagine you’re a bit more concerned about how this person got the access to communicate with you the way they did.” Ern was good at making intuitive leaps. The relative urgency of this call became more clear.

“Uncle Ern \- “ Greg paused, then continued. “Nobody ever asks you for details about your work in Alice Springs, but we’d all just assumed…”

“That Uncle Ern was a Spook in a prior life” Ern responded with laughter in his voice. “Well, let’s just say, I remain a Man of Mystery”.

“An ‘International Man of Mystery’”, Greg quoted the family moniker for Uncle Ern. Though he was a respected and universally liked member of the Bennington family, nobody knew what he’d been doing for most of his life. He travelled endlessly and was sometimes difficult to contact. Somehow, he’d always managed to turn up on important family occasions… and sometimes just out of The Blue.

But it was Uncle Ern’s computer networking background that had captured Greg’s interest as he was growing up as Ern’s nephew. “The Network is the System” Ern had often said when referring to his chosen specialist field. With the rise of mobile telephony, Ern held senior consulting roles in the Telco industry. He was a frequent visitor to Canberra. And Washington.

“Well, I can tell you this,” said Ern, puffing on his pipe. “There’s not been a great deal of ‘new math’ working its way into cryptography in recent times. Promises of quantum crypto, sure. But, the last few years have mostly been about consolidation. A lot of the standards *are* looking a little old. But that’s not really the point is it?”

“No” answered Greg.

“You’d like me to have a think, make a few calls and generally have a sniff around in places that I’m no longer a part of,” Ern concluded. “At least to give you an indication if you’re dealing with a bad actor of some sort.”

Greg smiled into his phone. “That’s it Uncle Ern. I’m inclined to accept this @man stuff as being positive and interesting and a great opportunity.”

“But you’d like a second opinion,” said Ern. “No Bayesian Priors. Try and formalise the odds, so to speak”

Greg was silent, expecting the following.

“Well, there’s some limits to what’s possible there mate” said Ern, sounding ready to wrap up. “It might be a probabilistic universe, but life is a bit too complicated for…”

“.. Therefores” Greg jumped in, expecting a variation on this thought from Uncle Ern. “Don’t worry, I’m keeping my expectations in the fridge.”

Ern laughed, hearing another thought of his in this statement of emotional cool. “Alright mate. I *will*  have a look around. And I *won’t* ask you for a copy of the paper. Not yet anyway.

Ern disconnected after some best wishes and levered himself to his feet, stretching his arms and shoulders. *I’m too old for this shite* he repeated to himself.

## Learners Permit

Adrian sat for a moment listening, before shutting off the engine. Silence. No ‘running on’. He smiled, looking around the interior of his old ride. Only human to anthropomorphise a machine. *If Japanese rocks can have Spirit, old Toyotas can have personality,* he thought. He took an inhale of the old car smell, much more pleasant after the clean up and washing the sheep skins tied to the seats. Somewhere between consciousness and dream state, Adrian said goodbye to his old vehicle.

He stood and closed the drivers door, pulling the L plates off the dashboard and fitting them in the frames he’d installed. 

“Hello mate”. It was Chris. He and Sparkes emerged from the house.

“Cuzz” said Heeni, hugging Adrian around the shoulders. “What’s this?” she pointed to the car. 

“A driving lesson, before I go.” Said Adrian. Heeni looked at him. Something was up. She decided to not worry about her apprehension. 

“Will you give me a lift downtown Adrian?” asked Chris. His face was impassive.

“Ok you too” said Heeni. “I don’t know what you are up to, but you are terrible conspirators.” They all smiled at each other, then laughed out loud.

“Sorry Cuzz.” said Greg. “I’m ready to head off to Sydney. I’m taking the train from Kiama. And I’d like you to look after my car while I’m away.”

Heeni stared at Adrian with mixed feelings. “Come on love” Chris said. “You’ve been at me about getting you some wheels for months. And you’ll have your licence in a few weeks.”

“Yep,” said Adrian. “And you won’t recognise the old beast. I’ve been doing some fixing and fiddling and most of the quirks are a bit less ‘special’ now.” He held the keys out to her.

Heeni was starting to smile. “Seriously?”

She sat in the driver's seat and adjusted the mirrors and slid the seat to a comfortable position. “Oh, I’ve fixed the steering height adjuster” said Adrian. Heeni unlocked and moved the steering column to where it suited her.

She pushed the clutch pedal and moved through a few gear selections. Chris climbed in the back seat. Heeni started the engine. “Hey, the exhaust noise… is gone”. She revved the engine a little. “You’ve been busy Adrian.” She felt emotion rising. Distant excitement of the freedom of her own car, gratitude to Adrian and impending doom of his departure.

“Your Dad too” said Adrian. “I kept this old thing running forever without fixing anything properly, but Chris and I spent a bit of time in the shed. Everything just came together. Parts from here and there. Oh, we got a safety inspection and registration for a year.”

Heeni breathed her mixed emotions. And smiled, turning to both of them. “Ok” she said. “Let’s rock and roll.”

\----

“I can’t believe the effort you put in for me Cuzz. And you too Dad. Thank you, thank you thank you both”. Heeni turned down the stereo. Yes, Stereo. Both speakers are working... Everything is working\!

She slowed down and parked near the train station. Adrian pulled a crammed backpack and a gym bag from the back seat. The train was waiting; not much time.

“The first day I get my licence, I’m coming up to see you\!” said Heeni. The drive had lifted her spirits. Her imagination was taking her places already. “I’m gonna call this car Cuzz… no, Adrian. Everytime I drive I’m gonna miss you and hang out to see you\!” She hugged Adrian long and strong around his neck. 

It was Chris who was wiping tears out of his eyes. “Come on Bro, you’re gonna miss the train”

## Laksa 

“I reckon it’s a One Time Password.” said Roy. “More like a pad of OTP. It’s gotta be. There has to be some shared secret. We just don’t know where and when that secret gets shared”

“Yeah, there’s got to be a handshake, an exchange”. Eric was mopping laksa out of his beard.  

Greg shrugged at him. “We’ll get a better idea soon. I think the *Promise* idea is very much the Java callback type promise. The rest of the stuff, the *Web of Trust*  idea, that’s not new…”

“Hardly. We’re basically doing that already anyway”. Roy continued “All our Web of Trust stuff is built on trust ratings. It’ll be much the same. Incoming\!”

Song smiled at the three guys. “How are you all? Who wants a beer?”.

Eric stood up, picking up his empty soup bowl. “Sit down Song. My shout tonight”. He headed to the bar. “Beer tax?” asked Song. Eric grumbled and walked off.

Song looked at Greg. “Pak says Hi. He’ll be home in a couple of days and wants to catch up”.

“Yeah, I really need to sit down with him,” said Greg. 

“You guys are all very mysterious”. Song continued \- “ I know it’s product launch time, but why all the stress and dagger/cloak stuff?”

“It’s the Curve Ball of curve balls” Roy answered. “We’ve had… we’ve got… There's a new idea we’re considering about how we establish trust in our system. It’s thrown us all for a loop.”

“We have to have a look at it” Greg was staring into space. He came back to earth and looked at Song. “That's why I need to talk to Pak-Lim. This could get messy commercially if we..have to redo a whole lot of code and delay the Premier's Department thing.”

“Well, I had a pretty good day,” said Song. “Little kids and dogs smiled at me and I smiled back. I finished hanging my show this afternoon, so time to sit back and wait for the opening. Can you guys make it?”

“Most def” said Greg. “Oh, I’ve got a friend landing in Sydney later tonight \- Adrian. How would you feel about another guest at your show Song?”

“Sure Greg,” she said. “Is Adrian another employee sort of friend?” she asked, smiling. “Yeah? What a surprise\!”. 

“Come on…” said Greg. “I know I mix the social and work world all the time...the consequences haven’t been too bad so far. “

Eric returned, holding four beers with the hands of experience. “Can we go outside? Karaoke is starting soon.”

“I’ll find a table \- hang on.” Greg went to the door onto Dixon St and looked around. “Come on” he beckoned them. 

They settled around a table for four and sipped their beer. “So, what did you do to deserve the Beer Tax Eric?” asked Song.

“I got cranky over some bullshit… some potential bullshit” said Eric, taking a long draw on his beer. “But I’m better now”. Laughter. 

“What’s your show all about Song?” asked Roy. 

“The It and the I.” Song answered. “The so-called mistranslation of Freud.” Song observed the blank looks and continued. “You’ve heard of the Id and the Ego? And had a dozen ideas about what these concepts mean to us? Well, there’s an argument that James Strachey spiced up the words a little \- ‘*Das Ich und das Es’*. Maybe all the tension between ids and super-egos is getting a bit lost in translation. You could read the German as “Where It was there I shall become”. This makes the whole idea more simplistic and appeals to the Jungian in all of us”

“I’ve heard this idea from Pak-Lim” said Greg. “He’s often talking about bits of ourselves being ‘split off’ by seeing them as Other.”

“The Shadow '' replied Song. “No doubt, I took some of this on from Pak-Lim. But I reckon there’s something there… at least for me anyway. It’s just as tricky as all fuck, the shadow stuff.”

“Why so tricky Song?” asked Eric. “We all have a subconscious. Can’t we just look for bits of behaviour that don’t fit in the Daylight world. I *was* taking notice during some of Pak-Lim’s oratories. There was a central idea, the way I remember it. Whatever you think of yourself as… how you define yourself… that’s a short list of where you have shadows. When I think ‘I’m a clever cookie’ I’m pointing at some shadow I have around my IQ”

“Welcome to the Upside Down” said Roy. Everyone looked at him. “Come on guys\! We’ve got to do some logic and reason to live in the world. How can everything be upside down? Isn't it like we have compartments for parts of ourselves? Daytime and Dreamtime? That’s a Pak-Lim thing.”

A passer-by interrupted the conversation to ask Greg for a smoke. He handed over his unlit rolly and made another for himself. “I reckon you’re pointing at the concept with your Stranger Things reference… and that’s why Song went with this theme for her show. Dreamtime language.”

“A story is like the Wind. It comes from a far off place. And we feel it.” Song was quoting somebody. 

“Who’s that from Song?” asked Greg. 

“It’s Lauren’s van der Post” , answered Song. “He wrote a biography of Jung. And a bunch of other stuff. Claims to have been a close friend of old Carl”.

“Claims.” said Roy after a pause in the conversation. He read from his phone. “Master Storyteller or Master Deceiver. That’s the headline from the Times.”

“Yeah, from a time before media take-downs were as obvious and frequent as they are today. I’d admit, some of van der Post's life story sounds a little suss \- spiritual advisor to Prince Charles? Come on\!”

“Again?” asked Eric, picking up the empties and heading inside. Nods of approval.

“I can’t wait to see your show Song,” said Roy. “It’s like, everything in my life is about ‘who am I?’, ‘how do we see ourselves?’, ‘what makes us Us?’. “

“You know the first thing I typed at a unix shell prompt?” said Greg. “ ‘id’. Tell me who you think I am.”

“‘id’” Song repeated. “So, how do you pronounce that?”

## Rosebush 

Tia is singing to herself as she pruned her rose bushes. The tune is Hymn-like, could be the recessional at a Saturday night mass.

She works along with the direction of the plant’s growth. There’s good northern sunlight in this part of the garden. The perfume from the vermillion is almost myrrh like \- nature magic calling on her oldest and timeless memories. 

The hum hymn is reaching its refrain. Aunty sings out loud…

*This Gravity, waves sand from sea*  
*On edge of tide I will abide*

*And trust my life*  
*To sense all strife*

*And write this tome*  
*That leads us home*

The garden in all its minimalism and plain-ness recalls other Garden’s and the calling of speaking people into fateful Knowledge. 

Aunty is opening up the centre of the most proud rosebush, removing weaker canes. No gloves. The discipline of caution. The thorns of practice. 

At the edge of the garden, there’s a bush that’s not doing so well. The soil has a bit more clay below the surface here. She’s spent time encouraging this particular plant to recover and thrive.

But the sunlight and topsoil and water and pruning are fighting a losing battle. 

Aunty is observing the Unlucky Rose Bush. She levers her body into standing position and stretches. She takes a few deep breaths. 

The Unlucky Rose Bush doesn’t have a thriving future. Poor drainage in this part of the garden. The soil is generally wonderfully around here, rich from the paltry Australian version of volcanic soils.

Aunty vocalises where her mind has arrived. “It’s time” she says, reaching for her spade. With one final regret, she pushes the shovel down with her booted foot. The rosebush says nothing as Aunty takes out a weaker plant for the sake of her stronger ones.

“You are a little wisp of soul, carrying a corpse”

## How many Selves

Pak stirs in his sleep. The cabin lights are still off, but there’s a glow from outside. He raises the window shade and greets the early dawn over the Tasman.

Long haul flights often arrive at dawn in Sydney. The flight path takes the aircraft south of the city and the urban areas. The last couple of hundred kilometres track the coast, flying over water. 

Pak chooses a window seat on the right hand side of such flights. On days like today, the vista is a gift. He ponders the concept of sitting with the pilots at a time like this. Curved earth rising to meet the metal bird. Promise in the transformation from starlit purple to early orange light.

The cabin crew announces the arrival to Kingsford-Smith airport in twenty minutes. Pak waves away the offer of coffee and pastry. Twenty minutes. A reasonable time to practise some breathing. He takes in the glowing horizon and shuts his eyes.

\---

Pak is deep behind his eyes when the gentle landing stirs him out of his morning practice. Passengers are turning on their phones. 

Runway One and the international terminal are in perfect orientation for the common long-haul approach and landing. As the speed washes off, they begin to coast towards the gate. 

Pak makes eye contact with his nearest fellow passenger. A kid, maybe ten years old. He’s smiling. Pak smiles back.

“Did you get some sleep?” Pak asks.

“I did\!” replied the young smiling face. “You did too. Whenever I looked over your way… you were just.. Gone. If I looked at you, I’d shut my eyes, then I’d be asleep. Gone too\!”

Pak laughed. “Thank you, that’s very kind of you to say. Sleep is very mysterious. I think it might be contagious”

“You’re right about that mister,” said the young man. “You seem like an expert sleeper mister”

“Pak” said Pak, waving. “Tom” replies the smiling ten year old. 

“Mr Pak. How did you get to be such a good sleeper?” asked Tom. 

The A380 passenger doors are open. Pak and Tom are standing near the aisle, ready to get moving. 

“Oh, it’s one of those “practice makes perfect” things.” Pak replied. “Some people are really good at it. Some have to do lots of practise”. 

They are walking across the airbridge. Pak smiles and makes eye contact with Tom’s nearby adult \- maybe his mother. 

“What kind of practice Mr Pak?” Tom asks.

Pak exchanges glances with Tom’s guardian. 

“Master Tom, there are a hundred practices. What seems to matter most is to have a curious mind. You are a young man. You have an advantage. Some traditions talk about a beginner's mind. That’s harder for many people as they get older”. Pak is watching Tom’s reactions as he speaks. Tom looks a little unhappy.

“I’m sorry Tom\!” Pak says as they move towards airport Customs control. “I don’t mean to be speaking in riddles with you. It’s hard not to when we’re talking about consciousness”

“And unconsciousness,” said Tom.

“Yes\!” said Pak. The passengers split into queues and made their way through customs.

Pak took a moment to say goodbye to Tom. “It was good to meet and talk with you Tom” said Pak, shaking Tom’s hand.

“Thanks Mr Pak\!” Tom was beaming. I’m going to practise some consciousness stuff.” 

## Sweat to blood

Heeni dropped her dobok on the floor near her gym bag and pulled out a ragged towel and wrapped it around her shoulders. The rest of the class were talking and dressing and wandering out.

“It’s good to have you back Sparkle\!”. It was Jane, a cheery middle aged lady. “How did you go today?”

“Well, it’s been a while..” Heeni replied. “I guess it’s like riding a bike”. She took a sip from her water bottle. “I think I’m gonna feel more like I fell off my bike later, when I cool down”. 

They shared some laughter. “Make sure you get some electrolytes into you,” said Jane. She dug around in her backpack and handed Heeni a couple of satchels. “These are pretty good.” Heeni smiled and accepted “gam sa ham nida”. 

“So, what’s news Jane?” asked Heeni. She opened one of the salt powders and mixed it up in her oft reused shop-bought water bottle. Jane eyed off the zombie PET bottle. Heeni noticed and let the moment pass through her.

“Oh, just the usual,” said Jane. “I’m training for a triathlon, hanging out with my sister’s family, trying to keep my chin up at life”. Jane was smiling. “I’m not quite in God’s waiting room yet.”

“Gawd, you’re fitter than me Jane,” said Heeni as they walked out the gym doors and downstairs, the institutional smell of the YMCA being wiped over by mops and lab-made pine esters. 

A few of their fellow students were gathered at the door, suggesting a beer and some dinner. 

Heeni excused herself and thanked Jane, strapping on her bicycle helmet and waving goodbye. 

“Two more weeks,” she said to herself. She imagined herself driving instead of cycling, music blaring, the windows down. 

It was still an hour till dark. She took the main road and kept clear of the dirt roads. There were steep hills on any route home. She sensed the lactic load in her thighs and calves. 

She was on the nasty stretch of the ride home now. A steep hill, blind corners. Australia might be a first world country, but secondary roads in rural areas were really just long sections of patched up pot-holes. 

There was a vehicle right on her tail, apparently not game to pass her. The road’s shoulder here was completely washed out forcing her to occupy part of the traffic lane.

Her legs burned as the hill became steeper. Noise \- like a jackhammer in the distance.

 She knew this road. Nowhere to stop. The driver behind her would just have to take a breath and be patient.

That didn’t appear to be working. Headlights from behind her were shining through her and onto the road ahead. A hairpin left curve approached. She was standing on the pedals now, her vocalising mind dropped away, breath taking over her being.

In the back of her consciousness, Heeni mapped the distant jackhammer again.

She heard the driver behind her kick back a gear \- the unmistakable sound of an automatic transmission responding to a driver flooring the accelerator pedal. 

The hill is very steep here. Seconds dilate as Heeni’s tailgate begins their passing manoeuvre. Heeni glances as a crossover SUV passes her. There’s a row of kids in the back seat, looking at her, waving and smiling. The driver waves apologetically as she sets up for the left hairpin, beginning from far on the wrong side unbroken yellow lane divider. 

The jackhammer is revealed. A fully loaded B-Double under exhaust brakes, headlights blaring and blinding. Time slows as 50 tonnes of blue metal shows no interest in losing momentum. The family SUV and cabover Scania are headed into Newtonian polar opposites. 

Space time seems out of options. Heeni stops pedalling and watches in pure consciousness.

The truck swings away from the approaching SUV, both vehicles diverting further out if ‘their’ lanes. The flat face of the truck seems aimed at Heeni’s heart. The SUV has disappeared from her sight. She can see the truck’s driver fighting with the steering wheel. As the microsends crawl, Heeni observes the B-Double’s fluid trajectory curve away from her. 

Milliseconds now. The SUV has emerged in the distance, violent over-corrections beginning to taper. 

The truck driver has entered ultimate consciousness. Miracle one \- steer away from a family SUV head on collision. Miracle two \- give the girl on the bike a chance to grow up and old.

A phase change progresses. The truckies' miraculous corrections are over. Over correcting. 

Nanoseconds now. Heeni stares from light years behind her eyes, watching the B-Double folding. It’s only a little kink between the trailers. Heeni’s pure consciousness watches the trailer kink become precursor to a jackknife. 

Picoseconds now. The truckie has managed to steer away the tiny human obstacle that would cause his rig exactly no damage. Another sharp bend approaches and Truckie instinctively leans harder on the brakes.

The B-Double kinks becomes a fold as the whole set of wheels moves into radical randomness. 

If time had been progressing, Heeni would have shut her eyes. No time.

No time. No velocity. No momentum.

Moment. Um.

## Central

“Bro\!” Adrian is calling out to Greg as he arrives through the sandstone arch under the clock tower.

“SO good to see you man,” says Greg, grabbing Adrian around the neck while applying nuggie to his mate’s skull.  “You’re looking well Adrian. Any dramas getting here?”

“All good Greg. A few harsh moments saying goodbye to my cuzzes and aunties. You know how it is…” Adrian trails off.

Greg looks him in the face. “That was really hard for you Bro \- I thought you’d be really happy to get back to the bright lights and late nights. What’s going on”

They’re walking downhill to Eddy avenue. Greg gestures to his bike, perched illegally on the curb a hundred metres away. “I’ll fill you in, Greg” says Adrian. “What I need now is a beer\!”

“Yep. How do you like this plan?” Greg asks. Come back to my office and drop your gear. I’m only 5 minutes away. I’ve got an invite for us to a gallery opening in Darlo tonight, it’s already started. It’s my friend's show. \`\`Free beer and hot chicks’’” Greg is quoting Adrian’s early assessment of the upside of gallery openings from early in this friendship.

“Let’s do it,” Adrian says, considering what to do with his baggage on their ride. Greg produces an elastic strap and mounts the duffle bag on the back mudguard. It partially obscures the tail lights. “We’ll be alright” says Greg. It’s only a few blocks. He unlocks a spare helmet and starts the bike.

\---

Roy is still at his desk as Greg leads Adrian into WhoComm. Greg does the introductions and wanders into his office with Adrian’s bags. Adrian is looking at Roy’s screens.

“Sorry Bro,” says Adrian. “I’ve been off the tools for a little while. And Greg’s got me curious and a bit excited about what you’re up to.”

“He does that, eh?” replies Roy, smiling. “Greg’s been talking you up a bit man. Says you’ve got bitstreams in the blood\!” 

Roy points and describes the code he’s working on. Adrian is holding back questions.

Greg is back within a couple of minutes, miming the sipping of beer. Roy locks up his machine and they wander out to the street. “Let’s grab a train” suggests Greg. 

“Way ahead of you brother,” says Roy, pointing to the approaching rideshare. In the back seat with Greg, Adrian asks about the show opening. 

“It’s my friend Song Lim. We went to Uni together,” Greg answers. 

He looks at Adrian. “No, friend-friend. Not a romance. But it is complicated”.

“Always with you mate” says Adrian. “Gimme the tweet version”

Greg replies. “Song’s Uncle is a bit of an Obi-Wan. He worked for DEC and Sperry back in the day. Bit of a legend as a coder and architect. Made a bit of a fortune as a hired gun over the years. Now he’s my… boss, partner. Let’s say, he’s the co-founder of WhoComm.”

“You’ve told me about this guy,” says Adrian. “Pak? So this lady is Pak’s niece.  Gotcha. Geez man, you really are Spider Man. It’s all a web of connections around you”

Roy turns his head from the front seat. “Nepotism is central around here. All one big family. Not so different where I come from. I think my dad was tangled up with a Malaysian ‘family business’ of some sort. It might have been a bit shady. A lot of my uncles worked in ‘the business’. But hey, I went along for the ride. Paid for my uni in Singapore and opened a lot of doors.”

The ride is over. Greg leads them to the gallery door. Adrian observes “Mmm. Across the road from the National Art School. Plenty of punters then”

## Lead bones

“Hello Sparkle,” said Chris. “How did you get along at the dojo?”

“Dojang Dad. Get it right\! You’ll piss off my Korean friends if you blend their martial arts with Japanese names.” Heeni ran water into a plastic tub and dunked her sweaty training whites, wringing them out in the laundry sink. “Well, I don’t need to kick myself for slacking off with the training. I feel like I’ve been kicked by a dragon.” 

“I’ve made some dinner,” said Chris, lighting the stove. “Get showered and we’ll eat”

Her phone chimed. It was Adrian \- *I made it cuzz. Bright lights\! I’ve landed at an art opening, lots of art students and posh people. You’re gonna love this.*

She replied. *Gee thanks for the envy Cuzz 😒. I can’t wait. I’m just home from my first taekwon class in six months. I feel like I died on the way home. Have fun\!\! Thanks for saying hello.*

Heeni looked at what she’d written. Something odd about that? She showered, standing under the warm until Chris yelled at her about wasting water. Towelled off, whe went for shorts and T-shirt, joining Chris in the kitchen. He was serving up his Creole red beans and rice. It was Monday after all. “Smells so good Dad”.

Chris smiled and they sat at the kitchen bench. “A bit more Arnold was involved here. That’s about the end of him. I’m thinking about getting a couple more piggies.” Chris dug in, shovelling with a huge tablespoon. “What’s up darl? Not hungry?”

“Heeni\! Where’d you go? You’re looking all fugue-y.“. Chris was getting concerned. His daughter’s eyes were \- elsewhere. “Darl\!”. He touched her shoulder.

She came back from wherever she was. “Sorry Dad. I found myself.. Remembering. Watching a pig being gutted and dressed when I was little. I was sick in the stomach \- I didn’t want to know… certainly didn’t want to eat.”

Taking a breath, Heeni smiled and dug into her beans. “It’s great Dad. It’s always great. And the hamstock is a killer.” She felt the hunger and slowed herself down a little. She told Chris about her taekwondo class. There was no problem picking up where she was, preparing for the Red belt. “It’s gonna take a while.” She picked at her food.

Chris was finished already. “I’m thinking about the memory, watching a mammal getting cut up for food. It was a rabbit in my case. I was seven or eight years old, and lived in town all my life. I’m at my uncle’s farm and we’ve short of food. The adults went spotlighting and shot a couple of rabbits.”

“It’s a strong memory,” said Chris. But, my uncle could see what’s going on. He had me do the cooking, and reminded me about Herbs and Stewed rabbit in Lord of the Rings. It worked out. I was hungry and the rabbit stew was awesome.”

“But, there’s a mark in my memory about this,” mused Chris. 

“Dissonance,” said Heeni. “Like the subconscious says Yes/No. *Jein*.”

“Are you ok to eat?” Chris asked?

“Yeah, I’m fine. I’m just going slow.” said Heeni returning to her red beans. “I don’t want to make a pig of myself\!”

Chris laughed. “I’m proud of your Dad-Joke-Fu my daughter.” He gave the obligatory shoulder punch. “One thing, though…. When and where do you have this memory of a pig slaughter? I can’t remember that. When you were little, we lived with Tia, on the edge of town. A few chickens. No pigs. I can’t see where that memory came from.”

“Neither can I.” said Heeni, dreamlike. 

## Face plant

Ern woke up, an electronic pulse in his ears. His face hurt. Was he lying on the ground, face in the gravel? 

No, he’d been asleep at his desk, face first on the keyboard. The computer was screaming at his random face-on-keyboard contact. He sat up slowly. It was dark outside. He had no idea what time it was. 

He pulled the keyboard cable out of the laptop. The alarms pulsed on. Tempted to hit the power button, he tried blinking himself awake. No. No idea what had just happened. As consciousness booted up, his senses came online. He tasted blood in his mouth. He could smell it too. 

Time to have a look at himself. Hands on the armrests of his desk chair, he tried to get to his feet. 

Pain wasn’t quite right. His whole body broke through the sleepiness with a ten alarm fire message. 

Brain chatter was considering possibilities. Did a bomb go off in his office? Heart attack? 

He slowed his breathing and tried to relax. Thought monkeys were swinging through his mind. Some were chimpanzees on attack. His breath transformed costal lung action to diaphragmatic.  Memories led him to count to four… breath out… and four.

The monkeys faded. “Forward breathing technique” said a character in his mind. The character was in a choir practice space. Ern had learned this diaphragmatic breathing here and pinned it together with many other breathing techniques.

The autonomic and the deliberate duality of the Breath, or the Spirit as people referenced breath through deep time.

He scanned his body as his breath slowed and nerves calmed. Everything screamed pain. Ern’s daytime brain wondered if he was so badly injured that his mind had over-ridden the pain amplitude. 

The screaming computer key-stuck sound ceased. He opened his eyes. Normal. Everything looked pretty ordinary. It was dark, the only light coming from screens and flashing leds on various bits of gear. He scanned his body. The internal alarms were still sounding. He turned his swivel chair and looked through the open office door to his home. Darkness.

He found himself rising to his feet. The wall of pain had no obvious centre. He took a tentative step. And began shuffling towards his bathroom.

A slight hesitation before turning on the light above the shaving cabinet. His visage in the dark mirror… didn’t look right. His hand on the light switch, thinking Corinthian phrases.

One more deep breath. Ern flicked the switch.

He saw his old self. The same fella that was shaving at this mirror, maybe twelve hours ago. Some negative space of the keyboard on his cheek. He opened his mouth. No blood. The taste had faded, as had the smell.

“You stupid old man” Ern said out loud. “Glad I wasn’t driving when I fell asleep at the wheel, otherwise this bad dream might have been a bit more \- visceral.”

He wandered through the kitchen and filled his kettle for a cup of tea. Back to the office. He switched on the lights and looked around. All he saw supported the thesis that he’d fallen asleep at his desk. He plugged in the keyboard and woke up his sleeping screens.

There were a couple of messenger apps open and an “are you sure” message box. The “are you sure” was referring to a journal entry, considering Greg’s call and Secrets with Symmetry. He read the last few paragraphs and worked out where he was up to in the journal. The modified time on the journal was 5:39pm. The whistling kettle called him back to the kitchen.  

The kitchen clock showed just after 3am. Ern made tea in a small metal pot and thought about his appetite. Not much there. He opened his bread basket and sawed off some sourdough and considered the smell of toast. Butter. Vegemite. Cheese.

Bob would be awake now, in DC. Ern shuffled back to his office and ate his toast while checking new sites and message feeds. Public sites. Specialist sites. More spooky sites.

He sent Bob a ping via their public emails and logged onto a shell that he and some of the retired operatives used for casual chat.   
![][image1]

Ern disconnected from the session and expunged the trail of digital breadcrumbs left by the chat.

There was a familiar scratching at the door. Ern got to his feet \- the wall of pain was a distant memory. He opened the back door.

“Pugsly\! Where’ve you been”. The blue heeler looked at Ern. He sniffed his human's hand and blew a full breath on the ground. Ern noticed the early birdsong. Dawn wasn’t far off. He filled Pugsly’s water bowl from the tap on his back wall. “Hungry?”

Ern walked towards the kitchen, where some leftovers waited in the fridge. Pugsly followed to the stoop and halted. “Come on mate, you can come inside. I might have checked out a few hours ago.” Ern waved and tried again. He gave up and went to the kitchen, pondering Pugsly’s inbuilt refusal to enter a human abode. From the week old puppy, Pugsly would almost never come inside… not just here either. Anybody’s home was off limits. Garages, workshops and the front seat of Ern’s old utility were in a different category for some reason.

Ern setup his moka pot on the stove as the tub of leftover lamb chops and veggies rotated in the microwave. 

He carried the dog food to the back door, Pugsley bristling with anticipation. Ern scraped the leftovers into Pugsly’s food bowl and scratched his hound’s head and neck as Pugs went to town on the contents. 

“How do you feel about staying with Mike for a few days?” Ern asked Pugsly.

## Galleria

Roy waved a ‘no thanks’ to the offer of champagne at the gallery entrance. He was staring at the largest painting on display, wandering towards it with fascination.

“Two please,” Greg said to the gallerist. He looked at Adrian, who was smiling and scanning the installation. “Bro\!” Greg shook Adrian out of his trance. “Come and meet the artist”

Song was talking to attendees, shaking hands and smiling. Greg caught her eye and she smiled and waved. 

Greg and Adrian sidle up to the group around Song in that casual ‘line up’ that happens at show openings. Adrian’s eyes were drawn to the walls of the installation. Song was wrapping up the conversation.

Greg met her and offered a clink of wine glass. “How are you feeling?” Greg asked. “Butterflies?” Greg turned to introduce Adrian.

Adrian was transfixed to a set of paintings in front of him.  He was walking slowly, taking it all in.

“That’s Adrian,” said Greg. “I think he likes what he’s seeing. And he’s forgotten his manners.”

Song smiled. “That’s the kind of compliment I like most,” she said. “No chit chat. Just a bit of mesmer\!” They watched Adrian walking into trance and laughed together.

“I’ve got a surprise guest coming along tonight,” Song said, keeping her face expressionless. 

“Oh, why is it so mysterious?” asked Greg.

Song had another set of groupies lining up to speak to the artist. Greg took the moment as his turn to take in the show and went for the Slow Walk.

…

Greg ran into Roy in front of a panel titled “Wisp of Soul”.  
Roy mused..”I’m glad that I had a chance to talk to Song about this show before I arrived. It might have been to mysterious for my otherwise”

“Feel a bit out of place with the art appreciators Roy?” asked Greg. “I’ve known the feeling. It took a bunch of art installations for me to lose that sense. These days, I’m happy to look and see what feelings come up. And they don’t always show themselves.”

“Well, my feelings are mostly saying ‘awe’,” said Roy. “It’s like looking at the outside of being awake from a dream”

“That’s not a bad way of putting it.” It was Adrian, who appeared to have returned to earth. 

Greg’s phone chimed twice.

“Eric’s not gonna make it,” said Greg. “He says he’s onto something important.”

“More like he’s scared of being clueless at the gallery,” said Roy.

\----

Adrian saw a chance to introduce himself to Song and walked to shake her hand.

“Hi Adrian. Welcome back to the big smoke.” Song said. “How long till you dive into the WhoComm pool?”

“Tomorrow I’d say,” Adrian answered. “I freaking love this show Song. I was spirited away the minute I opened my eyes to the big painting near the entrance. Can you tell me about it?”

The room was buzzing with conversation, white walls and the wearing of clothes.

## Web of Trust

Eric was sitting back in his chair. His eyes were strained and his hips stiff from sitting.

He’d found a familiar thought that pointed to the Secrets with Symmetry idea. The Phil Zimmerman Web of Trust in one of its more advanced forms, the Gossamer Spiderweb of Trust. 

[www.gswot.org](http://www.gswot.org) was looking a little unloved. No indications when it was last active or updated. It was basically a structure for building a network of trusted identities, looking very straight-arrow. None of the complexities of trustless-trust of distributed ledgers. Best of all, no sign of commercial benefits that seemed to bring out the worst in people.

Eric pulled a page off the rarely used printer. He still found value in using a pen on paper, especially to teach, or walk people through a concept.

![][image2]  
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Web\_of\_Trust-en.svg\#/media/File:Web\_of\_Trust-en.svg

Secsym is how he’d started to think of the @man paper. One way to look at it was… the promise of webs of trust, with emphasis, with the emphasis on the plural *webs.* A promise to be able to instantiate a web of trust in real time. Further promises of interactions; webs of webs, also able to be pulled into existence in real time. And no need for revocation as all of the instantiations would be ephemeral.

Eric was recalling the day he read Phillip Zimmerman’s essay, “Why I wrote PGP”. Eric had been a teenager at the time, lucky enough to have dial-up internet. 

Zimmerman’s essay had disturbed him. It points to the difficulty of getting the ciphers right, the reliance on giant shoulders of the past. While Pretty Good Privacy was now so widely adopted, it was thought by some that the veracity of the code was questionable.

Eric had downloaded the source code for PGP and built it on his family’s PC. He’d felt like a rebel. He read all of the essays at philzimmermann.com.

Zimmermann had found himself running afoul of the System. His attempts to spread the word had attracted the attention of government Three Letter Agencies. He’d also crossed a line by ‘borrowing’ the Rivest Shamir Adelman public-key cryptosystem in his PGP 2\. This turned into a criminal trial, where Zimmerman was likely to lose all the personal freedoms he’d been campaigning for.

The criminal trial was partially driven by RSA taking action about their algorithm being nicked. Zimmerman quotes an executive from RSA at a lecture Eric had listened to: “It’s not good for business to sue a folk hero”. So, instead RSA encouraged a criminal trial to discourage other freedom campaigners from trying the same approach.

Eric stared at the Web of Trust diagram and considered possibilities. If secsym lived up to the claims, people would be able to use real time webs of trust to prove identity, not needing and maybe eschewing the formalities of government identity. Imagine how much the TLAs would just love that \- Not\!

More exciting and more concerning was the *persona* concept and services in the secsym paper. The idea was, a citizen could establish a web of trust and use it for all their Citizen interactions. They could also take on a *persona*, which was trusted by various webs of trust, with very little possibility of tracing the *persona* back to the citizen. Ephemeral trusted Identity.

Eric was recalling an interview with Christopher Boyce, who sold American secrets to the USSR and spent 40 years in prison, many full years in solitary confinement. 

He shut his tired eyes and tried to slow his breathing and heart rate. He briefly played with the idea of quitting the job at whocomm and ‘doing a runner’. He had a Swedish passport and relatives in Gothenburg. 

He glanced at his messages. Nothing jumped out at him. It was nearly 9pm \- the gallery crowd would be at the Darlo Bar by now. 

He showered, put on clean clothes and headed to the Darlo.

## Fresh Air

Adrian and Greg had moved on from champagne to bottled beer. They were on the street in front of the gallery, resting their beers on a neighbour’s brick fence. There was friendly chatter around the other smokers and vapers who’d wandered out the front. Nicotine still proved popular with art students.

“This is a life Bro,” Adrian said to Greg. “FBaHC”. They laughed and clinked bottles. 

“I’m looking forward to getting into it tomorrow Bro,” Adrian said. “Want to give me a bit of a picture of what needs doing?”

“Well,” said Greg. “Things are moving fast. They’re also changing fast.” He was rolling another smoke as he tried to give some clues to the last few days. “We were getting really close to committing to a code release. A government job. The deal is signed right up to a trial stage. Milestones and progress payments.”

Adrian noticed a middle aged gentleman who was arriving at the gallery, dressed sharply. They made eye contact. Something… familiar in that glance.

“It’s decision time, tomorrow I think. All the secrecy makes this tricky. We’ve got to talk it over with Pak. And parts of this are… impossible. We need to be face to face.”

“Don’t speak of the devil, lest he appear” said the new arrival, meeting Adrian and Greg face to face.

“Pak Lim\!” Greg jammed his beer in Adrian’s hand and grabbed Pak in a bear hug. “You really do know how to make an entrance. I am so pleased to see you face to face.”

Greg made the introductions. “Welcome on board Adrian. Seems you picked an interesting time to join us. So much going on.” 

Pak excused himself and went to greet his niece and check out her show.

“Incoming” said Greg.

Mae and Vicky were arriving together, dressed up for the occasion. Hands are shaken and names exchanged.

Adrian was glowing inside. It wasn’t just the beer. The return to a city from a rural town. The smiling faces. Adrian was an openhearted person. He was with a bunch of openhearted people.

“Come on,” said Greg, heading inside. Pak and Song were standing together in the middle of the room, beaming at each other and the room.

## Red dots and red faces

“I’ve gotta keep myself calm,” said Song to Adrian as they walked together from the gallery. “That could go to my head if I’m not careful.”

“Oh, I reckon it could. Everything about the last couple of hours felt like… better than fiction. Like, the real article. Life, being weird and wonderful. And timely.” Greg was talking from his depths. He came back to Here and Now. “So, why not think about the sell-out show as one of those synchronous things that Life deals out sometimes.”

“I’ll try that. I am trying that,” said Song. They were already arriving at the Darlo Bar. “Trying not to take it personally. I think that’s a pretty useful perspective…. It works all the time. When life sucks. When things are steady. When things are wonderful. Life effing NOW\!”

“Hey, we’re all still humans. Mammals. Dolphin’s cousins,” said Adrian as they walked up the stairs. “And dolphins ride waves. Have you seen that? I’ve been in the surf having a bash and seen dolphins riding the same waves.”

They arrived at a corner table of the outdoors bar, full of the gallery crowd. Pak Lim and a member of the bar staff were carrying bottles of champagne to the table.

“Surf’s up sister\!” said Adrian, calling the moment accurately for Song.

\---
