Alcohol was doing its positive mood amplification thing. So many conversations. Vicky tried to say goodnight and was met with a good humoured protest. “School night, schmool nite”. Greg passed his tobacco around enough to run out. Laughter drowned out the music. 

“Hey\!” shouted Mae, outside her quietly spoken demeanour. “Over here Eric.” The crowd’s generous welcome brought a bloom to Eric’s cheeks. His anxious evening seemed to fall from his shoulders as the same Wave picked him up and took him along for the ride.

“Click” said Greg, touching glass with Pak Lim. “Now we’re all here\!” 

“Yes, and I’m feeling very happy about that, “ replied Pak, holding back his first thought; *almost all here.* Adrian was making dolphin noises to an appreciative bunch of people nearby. 

“Tonight, we’ll put our concerns aside,” said Pak. He noticed Eric looking his way. Something urgent in his eyes. “I think Eric needs to tell us something”

\---

Two hours later, the core of the WhoComm crowd were sitting at Tropicana in the Cross, drinking midnight coffee. Adrian delivered a toastie to Song, addressing the squirly feelings in her belly. The tide was receding in the evening. 

Eric had his chance earlier to brief Greg and Pak Lim on what had gone down tonight. Once it was out there in the open, Eric had relaxed. Despite this, it was going to be tough to let the news slide into sleep.

“Let’s go back to the office for a while,” Pak said to Eric. “I need to read this paper for myself. I can see, everyone here is ready for bed soon.” 

“Not me. Not yet.” It was Mae, who’d carefully slowed down her drinking hours ago. “I feel like I’m in the middle of this.” 

The table broke up as hugs and final wishes subsided. Adrian and Song stayed a bit longer.

“Those guys are pretty keen, aren’t they?” said Adrian. “Weird. I don’t feel drunk at all.. But don’t ask me to learn some new cryptography at the moment.” 

“There’s another Wave rising around them… around all of us,” said Song, focused on the middle distance. “People are on \- a bifurcation Wave. My gut says, these forks in the road are coming out of being online all the time.”

Adrian pursed his lips. “Yeah, it’s been getting pretty weird lately. My Aunt says she saw this coming”.

“How so?” Song asked.

“She calls it the Unexpected Side Effects. Says it’s a lesson from life. She’d always talk about industrial agriculture. Feed the world, save the hungry. Decimate the insect population in the process”.

“The Road to Hell” suggested Song. 

“Yeah. I was on the Hopeful side of connecting the world, letting them communicate. I didn’t see any of the Tribes forming, the Populism”

“It’s a worry isn’t it. Irony isn’t just for Hipsters. Peace makes War.” Song was starting to yawn.  

Adrian was typing on his phone. “There’s a word I need right now…. ‘Enantiodromia, the tendency of things to change into their opposites’. I’ve thought of it more like the pendulum swing. Big swing left leads to a big swing right.”

“Yeah, that fits. I didn’t know that word.. En ant...?” Song asked.

“It’s a made up word.” Adrian replied. “Carl Jung put it out there.” He typed again and read a quote “‘the emergence of the unconscious opposite in the course of time’”.

“Well, do I feel dumb right now,” said Song. 

“Why’s that?” asked Adrian. 

“My show\! The ‘It and the I’. I’ve read a fuck-tonne of Jung, about Jung. But I don’t know that word. I love it. Especially the second definition.”

“More from Wiki” Adrian read on…”’this characteristic phenomenon practically always occurs when an extreme, one-sided tendency dominates conscious life; in time an equally powerful counterposition is built up which first inhibits the conscious performance and subsequently breaks through the conscious control.’"

“Cathexis” Song mused. She smiled, a little wearily. 

“Should we swing out of here?” asked Adrian. 

## Turbo Prop

Greg’s replies came through after midnight and Ern woke up to the notification. He’d jumped out of bed with the agility of a much younger man.

By 5am, Ern had booked a flight to Sydney with a small regional airline. He wrote to his sister Emma, filling her in on his own situation and travel plans. She’d called him at 5.15am and offered to give him a ride to the airport. Mike would be overjoyed to take Pugsley for a few days. 

It’s just after 6am now. Ern sits with his coffee in the airport lounge. He tuned out the airport announcements as he checked his messages. It looked like Greg as on an ‘all-nighter’, updates arriving through the witching hours. He read some news sites. A freeze in world credit markets over Libor problems. Swine flu has led to the culling of millions of pigs in China.  Taiwan terminates relations with The Solomon Islands, playing the Dollar Diplomacy card. 

The Sydney flight is open for boarding. Ern has taken this early flight to Sydney many times. He’s still amazed to see how popular it is. People doing the work commute, be in Sydney by 8am. 

Ern smiles as he observes the Saab 340 that will take him to Sydney. It’s at least 35 years old. It was designed for a 20 year service life. Gotta love the design and engineering that survives so well. There are so few new turbo-prop aircraft these days. It’s a niche that stays filled over time.

There’s a spare seat on the single-seat row near Ern’s allocated seat. A No Show. As the flight attendant closes the door, Ern catches their eyes, pointing to the spare seat. The attendant gives him a T for timeout. The right hand side engine is turning over.

The flight attendant returns from the cockpit and gestures Ern to the spare seat. That’s an improvement. Somehow, airline booking systems seem to know how to place large bodies next to each other.

The RHS engine lights up, the whiff of avgas arrives like an olfactory friend. Ern buckles up and enjoys the whine of turbines as the LHS engine lights up. 

Ern watches the grass around the airstrip combed by the propeller wash. The pilot has pulled on the brakes and is running each engine up to near max power. Ern is curious. He’s an observant guy and is surprised to observe this. It’s been years since these pre-flight engine tests were a part of the process.

With the engines tested, the brakes are off, propellers pitched fine and the whine disappears into a growl. Ern feels his emotions rise in what feels like tears. This happens to him on all of the thousands of flights he’s taken. Leaving Home. Going Home. The thrill of flight. Each individual engineer, technician and their private lives, combining to make this minor miracle a Daylight Reality.

The growl becomes almost savage as they lift off over Coffs Harbour and climb over the Tasman. 

## All Nighters

Greg hung up the call. “Roy is on his way. He’s going to bring coffee”

“Yeah, that’s exactly what we need,” said Eric. “I’m already so wired. *Nessun Dorma.*”

Pak Lim was deep in thought. He picked up the secsym paper again and turned some pages. His soul was somewhere else.

“You don’t look too washed out Pak,” said Greg. “A ten hour flight, an evening of drinking and a vigil of crazy maths”

Pak remained silent, reading. Greg was rolling a smoke. 

Pak held up his hand. “Just before you go outside Greg. Have you thought about what might become of our enterprise here, if we adopt secsym? We will be copied and forked all over the net. In a month, we will have nothing unique to offer.” 

“I’ve been thinking about that. We *will* have the first mover advantage. We’d also be in a prime position to get this method adopted and see how far it could go.”

“We will be very busy.” Greg concluded, hanging the cigarette between his lips.

“Do you care if WhoComm needs to become a not for profit?” asked Pak. “A charitable organisation?”

“A religion?” Eric quipped, sarcasm hard to resist in his post-exhausted state.

“Pak, I’m not the one who put the big money at risk here,” said Greg. “To answer your question, I’d say Yes. We need to adopt secsym. We’ve been given a great opportunity here.”

“Gents, aren’t we getting a bit ahead of ourselves?” Eric said, compelled to reign Greg in. “There is *definitely* something intriguing in this idea. But a few tests don’t amount to the years of testing that’ll be needed.”

Eric took a breath and continued. “Look, this hasn’t really sunk in on me yet. Since yesterday, when I first tried the *persona* service.. Since I started thinking about the implications…”

“I’m scared. How long till some government spooks come at us to take the idea, all our assets. Will they be happy with shutting us down? Will we need to be silenced or doxxed?”

“Look, I hate admitting this,” Eric was arriving at the truth. “Last night, before I came to find you guys. I thought about packing up and running. Getting as far away from secsym as possible. Leaving the country.”

Silence. An electro-mechanical clunk announced the building aircon was starting up.

The office door opened. Roy was arriving with a tray of coffee cups and the welcome aroma of toasted croissants. Roy appeared rested and clear eyed. “God, it looks like you guys have been at a War Cabinet meeting.”

“Not quite,” said Pak standing up. “That comes next. Eric, wake up Mae. We’ll regroup in ten minutes.” He looked at Roy. “Can you have a look over this and try and take it in?”. Pak was gesturing at the screens they'd been sitting around. “We need a fresh set of eyes on what we’ve seen.”

“Come. Let’s smoke Greg”. Pak and Greg headed for the door, coffees in hand. A sleepy haired Mae met them on her way to the bathroom. Smiled. Said nothing.

Roy looked up from the screens at Eric. “Can you give me a clue on what I’m looking at here?”

Eric sat next to him, grabbing pen and paper. He drew out this state diagram.

“This system is meant for individuals and group membership. There is a group role \- *founder*. But otherwise, all the actors are peers.”

“Individuals can *associate* with secsym. There’s a handshake… It's kind of like a Shamir’s Secret thing. The *associate* shares a self-signed secret with the new *association*, the individual.”

“So, this is you. You’ve associated. You receive this key \- it looks pretty much like a .pem file.”

“You’ll get a browser redirect that decrypts the key and yields your part of the Shamir secret. Then you get this form… you can pick which bits of identifying info. But none of that’s required. Only one thing is required. I’ll show you. Let’s do it.”

Roy was picking up his phone. “No,” said Eric. “Use the computer. No IMIE or MSN. You’ll see.”

Roy looked uncomfortable, as he stepped through the process in a firefox session. “What am I signing up for here?”

Eric took a breath. “Don’t worry, I had the same reaction. We’ve all looked at it. You’ll be diving into the same ocean as Pak, Mae and Greg are already swimming with me.”

Roy typed and clicked and sat back. He was looking at the simple form presented on his browser. “Name, DOB… What the hell? It looks like any old contact or sign up form.”

“Look again,” said Eric. He pointed at a checkbox at the bottom of the screen, labelled Primary. “That’s the only required field.”

“Go on\!” Eric said. Roy ticked Primary and hit the archaic style submit button. A second later, his screen displayed a QR code. 

“Take a copy of that… no, a photo. I’ll print a copy too.” Eric grabbed the mouse and sent the QR code to be printed.

“So what have I got here?”. Roy decoded the QR code with his phone. 

“Don’t worry about that for a second, said Eric. He was operating his phone. A Signal message chimed on Roy’s phone. Roy hesitated. 

“It’s a group invite. To the WhoComm group,” said Eric, pointing at the payload in the URI. “Go on\!”

Roy hit the link. He was presented with another archaic webform, a large text box. Two buttons. “Sign” and “Decline”. Eric was on his feet, fetching a page from the printer nearby. 

“So, there is basically no client code in any of the pages you’ve seen. No ‘request access to camera’, no ‘permit this’.” Eric looked at Roy’s phone… “Actually, that’ll do. Can you save that decoded QR contents to… to somewhere that lets you paste it into the Sign form?

A couple of clicks later, Roy had pasted in the code contents and was mouse-over the Sign button. “I still don’t understand.”

“Don’t worry Roy. Think about it. How much identifying information have you given away in the last 2 minutes? None. You filled out a webform on a computer… that’s Mae’s login we’re using right now. You didn’t run any code on your phone at all. You could have done that decode with your phone offline.”

“What are you worried about?” said Eric, the lack of sleep was eating through the last shreds of patience.

“I don’t know what I’m worried about. But, I don’t understand what I’m doing.” Roy looked uncomfortable. 

Eric breathed in and out slowly, calming himself. “Look, this is gonna make a lot more sense in a few seconds. I understand. There’s something going on here that looks impossible. But when you see it…”

Roy stepped to the edge of his doubt \- and jumped.

The form was replaced by an understated success message, in all its Courier 10 glory.

“Welcome to the WhoComm Group”

“So? What just happened? Eric. Help me understand\!” Roy asked.

Eric was smiling and slide-typing on his phone. “I’m gonna use the phone camera and QR decode this time. It’s too cool”. He scanned the paper copy of Roy’s *associate* key. 

“I’m sending you a key signing request,” said Eric. “You respond in kind, and we can act as trust partners if you ever lose your Primary Id. But that’s not the cool part.”

Roy went through the motions of signing Eric’s request, using his phone this time. His doubts were fading a little.

Eric moved to a nearby laptop and logged it out. “This laptop is running a little PAM module that lets WhoComm people authenticate.  Go on.” Eric offered the laptop to Roy.

Roy saw the unfamiliar icon near the Username box. “@ss”. He clicked it. The laptop’s camera started and presented him with a scan box. Roy followed his intuition, grabbed the paper copy of his *associate* QR code and waved it in front of the laptop camera. 

The logging process completed and Roy’s profile appeared on the laptop.

Roy was dumbstruck. Eric was laughing out loud, clapping Eric on the shoulder. 

Mae arrived, wet hair brushed and tied back so tight that her forehead gleamed. “So you’ve got the authentication going. Sweet\!”

Eric was still laughing. Mae looked out the window, seeing Pak and Greg in deep conversation.

“Why do they look so serious?” Mae asked Roy, bringing him back from his trance. Roy said nothing.

Eric answered for him. “Oh, just deciding the future of WhoComm. And the world.  And us. Nothing serious.”

## New Daze

Adrian opened his eyes and blinked. The ceiling above him was unfamiliar. There was bright sunlight in wedges around the ceiling and an unfamiliar room. 

Song was sleeping alongside him, breathing quietly. 

One thing led to another. And another. Adrian closed his eyes and smiled at his wonderful life.

His thoughts were knocking on his consciousness. He turned over towards the edge of the bed, wondering where his clothes were hiding. A familiar buzz indicated that his phone wasn’t far away. 

Song stirred in her sleep. Adrian looked at her shapely shoulders and long hair. He followed his instinct and rolled slowly to wrap an arm and then a leg around her. 

“Good morning,” she said in a murmur from sleep. She wrapped her arm around Adrian’s hug and held the back of his hand. 

“Good morning to you too,” replied Greg. “How are you feeling?” His embrace wrapped her more closely. Song fumbled at her phone on the bedside table. She made an unhappy sound.

“Feeling? I’m feeling warm and happy and sleepy” said Song. “But, it’s after 8 o’clock and I need to get moving.” She turned to  face him.

“Yeah, I should too,” said Adrian, hugging her whole body with his. “God, I feel like *I* might be losing it… like you said last night. I feel like I’m having a wonderful dream… from when I arrived at the gallery last night.”

She turned and faced him. “Just go with it. That’s what I’ve decided to do”. She held his face.

“You want a shower?” Song asked. “The bathroom is shared here. I can’t hear the water running. You have to grab it when it’s free.” She rose out of bed and Adrian sat up.

Song pulled on a bathrobe and dug around in the lone wardrobe, returning with a towel for Adrian. He’s found his clothes. His boots were a bit more mysterious. It was a tiny studio, not many places for shoes to hide.

“Outside the door,” said Song, reading his confusion. “Come on. Before someone gets in the bathroom.”

## Harbour City

They were on final approach. Ern looked east over the Bridge, clogged with traffic. A perfect spring morning. 

There’s always some new construction in Sydney. Cranes dotted their way from Pyrmont to East Sydney. Ern smiled. The place still felt like home. He’d spent half of his life here. 

He synched up his seatbelt and shut his eyes, palms face up on his knees. His breath became deliberate, slow and deep. Ern had been taking landings in deep contemplation for many years.

The steward was announcing landing, while taking the jump seat. He looked around the cabin. The old guy, the big guy in the no-show seat, looked like he was sleeping. The steward blinked and looked again. “*Granddad?”*  he thought. “*He’s certainly got some miles on him. I wonder what his Story is?*”

\---

Ern took the train into the city. No point sitting in a taxi in traffic. He took a nicotine mint, putting aside the craving for smoke. There was an urgency in Greg’s last few messages. Time to smoke later.

His phone chimed. A video from his sister, Mike throwing a tennis ball to Pugsly. Ern smiled and dug around in his tiny backpack, finding his water bottle. “*May you never thirst”*  he recited Frank Herbert and took a mindful sip.

The train was packed. Ern squeezed against the window as a guy in a suit took the seat next to him. “Morning,” said Ern. 

“It is that” said the suit guy. “Don’t know about good, though”. He was reading news from his phone as he replied.

“What’s news?” asked Ern.

“Oh, just a frozen credit market. I keep wondering when something like this is going to blow up the money system.” Suit guy explained that he worked for Macquarie Bank.  
“How bad is it?” asked Ern. He noticed a teenage school kid listening into their conversation  from a facing seat.

“There’s a spike in the Libor rate. You know about that?” asked the suit guy. He didn’t wait for a reply. “There’s a tonne of credit that moves around the world overnight, providing credit to keep cash flowing. It’s done by loans. Banks around the world have lost trust in each other. The US central bank is buying up collateral that’s used for… sorry. This is a big deal to me. The details are boring…”

“Repurchase agreements. So, the Fed has to bail out the Repo market.” said Ern.

Suit guy looked at him. The old fella looked more like a retired motor mechanic than a finance guy. “Yeah, that’s right”. He looked at Ern with a face full of question marks.

“I read Zoltan Pozsar. Jeff Schnider’s the guy who really understands the money mechanics.” Ern dropped the names like they were old friends.

“Is it serious?” The question came from the youthful eavesdropper. 

Suit guy considered the answers. Ern piped up. “Don’t worry yourself about it mate,” Ern said with his most encouraging smile. “Money is like a computer game these days. If it breaks, it’s a software problem.”

The school kid nodded. “Yeah, I know. ‘Money Printer go Brrrrrrrrrrr\!’”

Ern and the suit guy laughed. The boy laughed too. 

## Decisions

Greg looked at his phone and swore at himself. He hit the dial button on his messaging app.

“Adrian. I’m so sorry Bro. Things have been a bit hectic. Where are you at? Where’ve you been overnight?”

“I’m on a train on my way into City mate,”Adrian answered. He passed over the question about where he’d stayed last night. “All my gear is at your office. Will I come straight there?”

“Yes mate. Give me a buzz when you get here and we’ll let you in.”

Greg hung up immediately and saw Uncle Ern’s message arrive. He responded with address details as he walked back to the group of WhoComm people crowded around the same monitors where it was taking place.

“It’s going to be crowded around here today. Adrian will be here in ten minutes. Obi-Wan shortly after” Greg explained. Roy and Mae had questions on their faces.

“Ern Benninongton, he’s my Uncle. He was some kind of government crypto spook in his career. His life is straight out of a Le Carre novel.”

Silence.

“I know, this is all so weird and moving so fast. I feel like \- I’m caught in, we’re caught in some kind of tidal wave,” Greg suddenly felt very tired.

“Something much bigger than us,” said Pak. “Even if we could fight it, or run from it, I don’t think it would make much difference. But each person must choose for themselves. For me, there is a raging river rising up around me. I am going to dive into it.”

He looked around the room. “You should take some time and make your own choice. We will respect your decisions. Life has thrown a fascinating challenge at us. You must choose it from your best idea of free will. If you decide you don’t want this challenge, we will stand with your choice and help you to move on to something else.”

Eric spoke up. “Count me in. I had my run-in with fear and doubt yesterday. I’m clear in my mind. I have to do this.”

Positive comments from all around the room. Vickie sounded a little unsure of herself. Greg’s phone notifications broke the silence. He headed downstairs to meet the new arrivals.

\--- 

Ern and Greg stood near the motorcycle parking lot, smoking. “Still mixing up the Erinmore?” asked Greg, enjoying the powerful incense of pipe smoke.

Ern nodded and drew in a lung-full. His teeth showed some signs of the tobacco addiction. Otherwise, the habit hadn’t left many of the teltails that long-term smokers wore on their faces. He briefly recounted the conversation with Bob. 

Greg took this in, saying “well, I don’t know how much that matters now. We’ve set up an environment and tried out the trust web. So far, it works pretty much exactly as described.”

“Hey\!” said Adrian. He’d been standing nearby for nearly a minute, waiting for Greg and Ern to take a pause in their conversation. He finally decided he’d need to interrupt.

Greg cut to the current scene of the story unfolding around them. “I hope you’re in for some excitement and stress Adrian. Pak has already made a call to the Premier’s department, indicating a delay. We’re gonna go for it, the secsym architecture.”

“Sweet as Bro,” Adrian wore his Kiwi expression with pride. “I feel ready for it.”

Greg looked at him. Insight cut through his tired and wired mind. “Friend-girl. Don’t worry Adrian. I’m kind of surprised. Song has a preference for girls. I’m not weirded out. Not even surprised, given the wave of change that’s gone on in the last few days.”

“Come upstairs and meet the crew,” said Greg, squeezing out the ember at the tip of his cigarette with his fingers, folding the butt end to toss into the rubbish.

“Travelling light Uncle Ern?” Adrian observed, amazed at the tiny backpack. “Always,” Ern replied. Their reflections came to meet them as Greg went to open the glass door to their office.  
Ern lagged behind for a moment as Greg and Adrian walked inside.

“Uncle Ern?” said Greg.

Ern immediately kicked back into daylight consciousness. “Sorry, my mind wandered off for a second there.” Ern said in a confident tone.

Internally, the confidence had been through an earth tremor. Ern’s reflection in the WhoComm office door was that of a young, tall woman. Her facial expression was completely blank.

He tried to let it pass.

They arrived in the office, finding everyone busy. Pak and Eric were in the conference room, drawing on the whiteboard and talking. Vickie was on the phone, talking to a customer. Everyone else, face down at their computer screens.

## Atlantic

Half a planet away, an Irish  fishing trawler was returning to Rossaveel. For the second week running, Canavan and his crew had worked through a night of natural light.

Luminesce algae are rare phenomena in this part of the Atlantic. Canavan had seen glowing phytoplankton regularly while fishing for salmon off the west coast of Washington state.  The blooms of bioluminescence were common in that part of the world and common closer to coastlines.

These giant blooms through the north atlantic were a radically bigger critter. And critters they were \- untold trillions of phytoplankton. There’d been much discussion and reading of wikipedia among the crew. It seemed the settled science indicated that the ability to luminesce was a defence mechanism. The glow would draw predators of the algal predators.

However, all the googling and discussion seemed to create more questions than anything else. Why were these blooms so far from the coast? The north atlantic blooms were generally seasonal \- spring and summer. So, why would this be happening so late in September?

Many of the younger crew were making connections with Climate changes.  Canavan thought this was a ‘bit of a stretch.’ Canavan would try reminding them that weather conditions and climate were different concepts, even though weather tended to drive the climate.

“It’s like the ocean's neurons.” It was Dave Savage, a student from NUI that worked on trawlers to pay his way. “Like an FMRI image of the ocean as a conscious entity.”

“Sounds like the DMT talking to me Dave,” replied Phil Deegan, the senior fellow on the crew. “We’ve seen this most nights lately, and it’s a first for me. I don’t know how to think about it or feel about it. It’s a change. There’s a change going on.”

“And we’re looking for a pattern to match it up to. Climate or Consciousness, it’s a Change.”

His words hung in the morning air. Sunlight would come and mask this change of a displaced and unseasonal ocean phenomena.

## October Change Fest

The WhoComm office was lit 24 hours for the next month. Greg led the engineering effort to adopt their IAM components to use secsym. Eric wrote cryptographic proofs. Mae setup ephemeral Trust Webs and Roy tried to break them or pull them down.

Pak and Ern ran the decision making and split the night and day ‘shifts’ between them. Pak was an owl by nature and quickly slipped into sleeping through the day.

The younger crew tried to be around for the dawn ‘handover’ every day. While Pak and Ern had many conversations in private, the younger Scoobie’s were inclined to watch from a distance and ruminate on what’s being discussed.

The more private matters would have seemed oddly philosophical and other-worldly.

“We are creating a defensible system of Identity that might do most of what we’ve dreamed of for… as long as I can remember.” Pak and Ern were sitting on milk crates at the office entrance, Pak drinking chamomile to help him sleep. Ern was on his second coffee and tobacco shots for the morning.

Ern nodded. “In the process, we’re reinforcing the ideas of identity that support all the delusions of a separate self.” Ern puffed on his pipe and considered. “Joni Mitchell,” he concluded.

Pak smiled at him, waiting for context. 

“Don’t it always seem to go, that you don’t know what you want till it’s gone…” Ern sang the line from  Big Yellow Taxi. 

“I think my niece has insight on this,” said Pak. There’s some kind of scale and complexity dynamics that are… acting like a phase change.”

“Ergotic change.”  Pak concluded.

“Ergodicity.” Ern stated.

They realised the near homonym after a few moments. Both men laughed.   
Mae watched from the 3rd floor window as the two senior gentlemen split up for the day.

“Sam’s coming up.” she announced. 

Ralph and Sam. This was how the younger crew referred to Pak and Ern during their morning meetings.

\---

Ern stood in the elevator without hitting the button. He was ok. He wasn’t ok. There wasn’t enough sleep in this routine. He’d been burning through his dwindling reserves in the last few weeks. 

He pressed the button for level 3 and recited one of his favourite words. “Nevertheless”

\---

Ern smiled his best morning smile at the crew. Adrian was coming back from the bathroom after a shower. His morning run left apples in his cheeks. “Matua kēkē.” he nodded to Ern.

“Tama.” Ern replied. He reached his desk and started unpacking a few gadgets. He greeted Roy “Selamat Pagi, Mae sawatdee don chao”. 

Mae had a deep appreciation for Ern’s manners and deportment. “He’s a cross between a Brown Bear and a nok hook,” she’d said. Mae gave Ern a few minutes to settle in and walked over to his desk.

“How did it go overnight?” asked Ern. Mae smiled and sat next to him with a sheet of paper covered in stats. 

The news was good. Ern and Mae had designed a bunch of use-cases to find weaknesses in the secsym protocol. They’d found a couple of minor issues over the last month.

Mae had used a group trust with The Atman to report what they’d found. The WhoComm crew casually referred to the mystery person as Atman or The Atman these days. 

The bugs were fixed in hours and replies always came with test scripts. 

## Too soon

Heeni ran to her old Toyota, now known as Adriana. She was feeling like she could leap over a mountain.

She’d smashed her Economics exam just now. Before sleeping last night, she’d read through a summary she’d written on tax policy and the notes were fresh in her mind for the major essay she’d just written.

She’d finished the exam paper nearly an hour ahead of time. Enough time to re-read her answers and make a few corrections. She handed in the paper 45 minutes early and ran out of the hall.

Enough time to get home and grab her gym gear. Enough time to wonder at how this rite of passage was now a Write of Passage or a Right of Passage. Some sharp focus had emerged in her in the last month. 

She started Adriana and played some tunes, singing along as her autonomic nervous system ‘just drove’ the vehicle. She hummed and looked at the distant road ahead. Two days till the Physics exam. Then, only Modern and Ancient to go. Three more exams and she could take flight into the adult world.

Her subconscious was playing with Physics terms; permittivity and permeability. She caught herself wondering where this fascination with electromagnetism had come from. 

And the Maths exams? Well, who knows how that happened. She’d raced through the standard Maths test, still expecting problems with the Extension Maths subject she’d held onto as a ‘filler’ unit.

The car with the young lady in the driver's seat carried on with its symbiosis. They soon arrived home. Heeni came out of her trance and stamped on the foot brake.

There was white hatchback parked next to Chris's old 4WD utility.  She recognised the car…. But she can’t remember the owner. Taking some deep breaths, she feels a familiar but distant ache in her belly.

*“Something bad is about to happen*” says her inner monologue. She switches the engine off and approaches on foot to disguise her arrival.

She can hear voices. The unmistakable overtones of sex. 

Genuine surprise arrived in her next.  On the sudden precipice of her father abandoning her for a lover, Heeni’s heart slowed and she ‘went cold’

Not frozen. Cold. She quickly calculated a way out. Disappear for now, till Chris’s visitor leaves. Till Chris leaves. Pack a bag. The Disappear from the South Coast.

She marvelled at her chilled demeanour as she crept back to her vehicle. She knocked it out of gear and gave it a push backwards, towards the front gate towards the slight decline that would let her roll out of the driveway. 

With the car moving at walking pace, she took the driver’s seat. She expertly aligned the back rolling car towards the cattle grid and gate. The steering was heavy, no power assistance. This came as no surprise.

Nor did her precise reverse turn onto the tarmac, facing Adriana towards the descent through Macquarie Pass. 

The car came to a stop as momentum gave into gravity. Very slowly, Adriana began to roll forwards, down the hill. She switched on the ignition and selected third gear. The speedometer read 10km/h, gradually rising.

She expertly clutch started the car and turned off the music, coldly planning her afternoon escape.

## Blank Page

Vicki stared at the bottom of the page she’d been writing and doodling on, a little bored with marketing video from Splunk, offering to take all your data and host it in the Cloud.

She’d written it as if it were an essay title. She could not remember where the partial ideas came from. 

It didn’t really make a lot of sense.

The title read *The Counterfactuals of Identity Protective Cognition.*

She imagined the line in print, in a Medium article, with an attribution *by Vicki Xu.* 

There was a buzz of talk and computing noises going on around her. They may as well have been an eclipse of moths trying to reach a light source through a pane of glass.

She stopped the video. Its contents were sliding off her consciousness and falling in the great Bitbucket that thieves of Open Source wrapped up and re-sold as original software.

She’d been reading about Constructor theory in physics a few weeks ago. It was some wild, inside-out approach to seeing the Physical world. She’d googled and read definitions of ‘counterfactual’. The meaning didn’t stick. She’d need to use it in a few sentences.

But what was this sentence and where the hell… did anything come from?

She googled away on IPC and read Ezra Klein’s “How Politics makes us stupid” article. A paragraph leapt off the page at her.

*In April and May of 2013, Yale Law professor Dan Kahan — working with coauthors Ellen Peters, Erica Cantrell Dawson, and Paul Slovic — set out to test a question that continuously puzzles scientists: why isn’t good evidence more effective in resolving political debates? For instance, why doesn’t the mounting proof that climate change is a real threat persuade more sceptics?*

She read the whole article and kept her rabbit-hole heading. Soon she was on [www.dailyevolver.com](http://www.dailyevolver.com), reading Jeff’s article. She found the related podcast and grabbed her headphones.

“I’m getting lunch and going for a walk,” she said, leaving the office. Roy and Mae waved and went back to their screens.

\---

Vicki sat on a bench in Sydney’s Hyde park, on the east side of the fountain near St James. She wrapped half of her sandwich and swapped out a water bottle from her backpack, leaning backwards, her sunglasses pointing into the Blue above. 

Sydney provides spectacular light in the mid seasons. She loved this time of year. Cool, bright and full of burgeoning summertime. 

She watched the canopy of Moreton Bay figs above and around her. The breeze stirred her memory. “*A story is like the Wind. It comes from a Far Off Place, and we feel it”.* She felt the sense of trillions of living things in the cycle of the air, the descending detritus of seed cycles. She was zoning. Zoning out.

Into Oneness. As promised, a sense of interconnectedness was never far away. All one needed to do was open oneself to it. The Oneness was Alone, but the Lone-ness was myriad with consciousness.

She pulled out a spiral bound notebook and pen, writing.

“*We participate in being We. I participate in We-ness.”*

*“I’m a Tree on the Land. The Land connects me to all other Trees. I cycle the Sun and Wind and Rain through the depths of my roots. They hold me firm in the storms as I exchange energy with all other beings.”*

*“My roots and leaves eat old death and rainfall. I slow the Entropy into other Lives”*

*“I found other Selves and We morph  into Us. To Them, Into Other. Life Eats Life”.*

She looked east to the sandstone grandeur of St Mary’s Cathedral and considered old rituals of seeds falling on the earth and being ‘reborn’.

“*There is no Them. There is only Us.”*

Satisfied, she folded away the notepad and nearly stood to walk back to the office. Her observant consciousness spoke up and suggested a few more moments of sitting. She closed her eyes and tuned out the human sounds.

Down below, below where she sat, a City Circle train rumbled subtly. Her mind formed images. Memory bought back a story of a haunted, unused tunnel. Some kids had ventured there and become lost. Something vague in the memory. General MacCarther’s Bunker in a disused tunnel. Secrets. Trains travelling beneath the trees as giant metal worms. 

Silence followed. Time ceased any arrowing. 

Eternity took no time at all.

Her eyes opened with Realisation. She knew what to do now.

She stood slowly and stared at the old gentlemen playing chess nearby.

Vicki found herself hurrying back to the office.

## Ventricles

Tia held her niece in a powerful hug. A long life never took the edge from heartache.   
“Oh, Sparkles, I am so sorry that you had to go through that. I won’t speak for Chris’ poor judgement or timing. It’s just too much for you to carry at the moment.”

Tia disengaged and looked at Heeni’s face. Something… wasn’t right. Nor was it wrong. Something wasn’t Her. There was almost no emotion on her face. Her eyes glowed with fierce energy, but it was… metallic.

“I think I know what you’re going to say, Aunty, “ said Heeni. “Give it some time, things will calm down and work out. Yes?” Aunty nodded. Heeni continued.

“I’ve made up my mind. I need to be a long way away. I’m going to stay with Adrian in Sydney \- we’d talked about me being his flat-mate months ago.”

“I’m leaving now. I don’t want to go home, not even to pack my stuff. I had to tell you what’s going on.“

“My mind is made up. I’ve never been so sure of anything in my life. It’s time to hit the road.”

Tia considered talking about the few remaining exams and Heeni finishing high school. She breathed it away, thinking about her own teens, where she’d run from home and family and been gone 10 years.

“I understand darling, I really do. When that drumming in your heart tells you it’s Time. I’m not going to get in your way, even though my heart is already breaking…” Tia mastered herself. “You better have a cup of tea before you go”

Heeni threw her arms around Tia’s neck and squeezed. “God, Aunty. How would I ever face my life without you? You’ve always let me be Me.”

Aunty was filling the kettle now. Heeni stared out at the rose garden, wondering if she’d ever see it again. She watched Aunty’s broad shoulders as she made the tea. There was no wondering required. This would be goodbye. The Goodbye.

Heeni marvelled at her steady breathing and heartbeat. She didn’t question it.   
Tia sat on her stoop and kept on waving, despite the old Toyota being hundreds of metres away.

It’d taken all her resolve to keep the pain in her chest and arm to herself. Heeni didn’t need another loss at the moment. 

She should call the Doc or go to hospital. She knew that. This was far from the first heart attack warning she’d experienced. Her work as a nurse left her well informed about the cavern of risk underneath her now. 

There was a short mental argument on calling for help. The doctors would save her life, get her sorted out and send her home. And lecture her about a low fat diet. “No more bacon sandwiches\!”. That wasn’t the worst. Was there much point in living without New Zealand butter?

Tia went with old habits and said the Rosary to herself. Then she lay down in her rose garden and went to sleep.

\---

Heeni hung up the petrol pump and went to pay and buy some munchies for the trip. She peeled off a $100 note from the roll that Aunty had insisted she take with her. “You got to love an Enabling Aunty” she said to herself smiling. “Gawd, I hope I grow up like you Aunty Tia.”

Returning with nuts, chockies and lots of water, Heeni sat half in the driver's seat, messaging Adrian. “*I’ve gotta see you as soon as Cuzz. I’m driving up to Sydney now, and should be there by 6ish. Sorry, but this is a Big Deal. I hope I can see you soon.”*

She stared at the dashboard. The Hood release lever was looking back at her. She pulled it and checked the oil and coolant. The brake fluid was half empty. She looked around for signs of a leak. Lying down near the front wheel well, she spotted a trail of mineral oil and peeling paint. “Fuck FUCK\!” she said out loud. She messaged Adrian again… “*This is gonna take longer than I thought”*

She looked around and spotted an overalled bear of a man, working under a hoisted old Land Rover. She walked over to have a chat with him.

## Keys

Vicki met Ern sitting on the milk crate that now seemed to live near their office front door. He was puffing on his pipe and leaning back on the brick wall, staring across the street. “What’s going on Uncle?” she asked.

“Vicki\!” he smiled, beginning to stand up. She intervened, appreciating his manners but empathic with his knees. She put a hand on his shoulder and sat on the ground next to him. His smile continued. “Tell me a story, Vicki,” Ern said. 

She replied. “There is no Them. There’s only Us. It’s the only Story we need. The only Story we’ve ever needed. And I mean We like all creatures, all critters…”

“All Sentient Beings,” Ern completed the thought for her. “Yep, that’s the Story of Us, of living things. We live on the edge of being With or Without.”

He looked at her, eye to eye. “You had an inspirational lunch then?”

“Hell yeah\!” Vicki said. “It’s hard to sum it up. I saw life anchored to the earth and dark metal worms tunnelling underneath. These were every-day fig trees and Tangara worms.”

“I’m more of a Red Rattler guy myself.” said Ern, lighting his pipe again. Vicki was laughing.

“I love that tobacco smell. What is it? It’s not the same stuff that you’re always smoking.

“The best of British,” Earn was replying. “There’s a pipe shop down near Ultimo that has a huge range. This stuff, this Condor brand… I haven’t seen it anywhere for many years. I read a review when looking for it online, years back. It described the scent as “slightly mildewy flowers, locker rooms, old fashioned barber shops and burnt toast”

They both laughed. “Well, I may now be an Enlightened Metal Worm rider, but I better go in and type something. What’s your metaphor for self right now Uncle Ern?” Vicki asked.

“The same as it’s been for as long as I can remember,” said Ern, gaining his feet slowly.

“I’m a Dead Person in Holidays”

## You’re hired\!

The WhoComm crew sat out on the Dixon Street edge of the Covent Garden hotel, moving into their second round of beers.  They’d left Pak and Ern in the conference room. Their conversation had become vague and tangled with ethical concerns about digital identity.

“What is it with Ralph and Sam?” said Roy with a tone of frustration. “We’ve worked through all the edge cases… things look really solid. We might be about to change the world for digital trust and it’s like.. It’s like they aren’t sure they wanna go through with it. Fark\!”

Adrian’s phone chimed a couple of times. He answered, threw the battered phone on the table. The phone didn’t seem to like this. It rebooted itself.

Vickie rarely drank beer with her colleagues, considering herself a bit susceptible to rapid onset drunkenness. She drained her glass and stood up to go to the bar and buy a round. “Well, I get it. I get the concerns that Ralph and Sam have. It’s about considering unintended consequences. The road to hell…” she trailed off, walking inside the pub.

Silence for a minute or so. Eric piped up with “I’m not sold on the mysterious root key either. Sure, everything seems to work. But, *how is it possible* for law enforcement to find potential bad actors? The Cold War, sleeping with the enemy way \- that’s the only style of answer I can imagine.”

Adrian’s phone chimed again. He swore and picked it up. “Give us a minute,” he said, walking away from the table and making a call.

Vickie returned with a tray of frothy schooners. Roy was speaking  in Pak-Lim phrases, not derisively. “‘Dive into the raging river of fate’. We dived already. We dove, if that’s the past tense. I’m in the effing river already. Why are we talking about getting out of the water now?”

Vickie sat down and took a generous sip of her beer. “That’s not what they’re on about Roy,” she said. “I think they’re more concerned about who and how many people get dragged into this river. The River’s a good metaphor I think. What ocean do we end up in? Is there a flood rising? Will we inadvertently get a bunch of lemming-like folks chucking themselves in this same River?” Laughter and beer sipping went around the table.

Adrian came back to the table, didn’t take his seat. “I’ve got to disappear for a while. My cuzz has just done a runner on her home life down the coast. She needs to.. I better..”

“You have to go to her \- now Adrian,” said Eric. “It’s ok. What’s the stress?”

“Song is on her way down here.” Adrian was tapping his phone on his chin, hoping something would shift in his head.

Greg spoke up. “Bro, go get her and bring her down here.”

“Go find her and see what she needs first,” said Vickie. “Maybe the last thing she wants is to talk about whatever happened. Go find out. Song will be understanding. Come back later, bring..What’s her name?”

“Sparkles. We’ve always called her Sparkles.” said Adrian, walking away from the nodding heads and encouraging words from the Table of Wisdom.

\---

Adrian stayed on the call with  Heeni, guiding her down Cleveland Street and towards the Goulburn Street car park. He saw his old ride turning onto Goulburn Street and waved her down.

They spent a full minute in a kerb-side bear hug. Heeni buried her face in Adrian’s tanned and freckled neck and shoulder. Her olfactory senses glowed with ‘familiar’ reactions. The hug ended and they held each other’s shoulders, speaking eye to eye.

Adrian opened his mouth to ask Heeni what was going on. He closed it again. Then said “What do you need Cuzz?”

Heeni smiled broadly and Sparkled at him. “God I love you Cuzz\! I want to.. I want a..”

“Want a Coopers Pale or two with my friends?” asked Adrian. Heeni hugged him again and said “Is the Bear a Catholic\!\!?”

They climbed in the car and parked it in the nearby multi-story. Adrian began outlining his first few months in Sydney. 

## Goodfare, AB

François parked the old Dodge near the workshop and listened to the Cummins distinct engine rattle. He shut down the engine and dug around for his gloves and beanie. It would be cold outside. Midnight. A couple of fine days on their way into winter. 

He climbed out of the truck and pulled on his jacket, gloves and hat. Not much steam in his breath, despite the cold. The air was sharp and dry.

In a few minutes of trudging towards the house, François could hear voices. The sound of an accordion rose in the air. Jerry must be sitting up late with the woofers and guests. He arrived at the fire pit to slightly inebriated enthusiasm. 

“How’d you go François?” asked Jerry. “I thought you wouldn’t be back for hours.”

François grabbed a beer from the nearby table and sat with the household. “All good. Coming back was so much easier, with no load. Good advice about tightening down the load Jerry. I stopped twice on the way and synched up the dogs. The tractor *did* move around a bit. And I’m glad buyer \- Jarrod, was there to back the trailer up to his shed. I didn’t want to try that\!”

Jerry thanked him for the long day’s drive. The farm was busy getting set up for the snow that could only be weeks away. 

François sat back and watched the aurora rising out of the north. “That is still so extreme,” he said. “Do you think it’s still building up?”

“I’ll be able to read a book by the light of the solar winds if this keeps up,” said Gunter, one of the woofers who’d agreed to stay over the winter. “I wish I could film this in a way that \- speaks my Awe over it”

Jerry hummed from his belly and lit a cigarette. “It might be one of those experiences… like looking into a whale’s eye. Or flying over an active volcano. When you’re there in person, it’s an emotional experience. Awe is the right word.”

“Death is the road to Awe” said François.

The fire crackled. “What?” “Huh?” Everyone around the fire looked at François.

“Oh, it’s a line from a film. The Fountain. A Shaman says this to the protagonist guy, who is trying to get past the Sharman to the Tree of Life”

“Tree of Life” Gunther stated. “How does that work out for the guy?”

“He drinks the sap from the Tree of Life. And uses it to heal a belly wound that would definitely have been fatal,” said François.

“The Tree of Life grows out of his body and he becomes part of the Garden”

The fire crackles. François has left everyone speechless. One of the old farm dogs is on his feet, aware of movement just outside of the firelight. 

“Probably another weasel heading to the chicken coup,” says Jerry, as eyes around the fire look together for movement.

There’s a squeal of pain that jabs the silence.

A Great Horned Owl appears out of the darkness, rising straight over their heads and the firepit. The weasel struggles pointlessly in the silent talons as another life-hunting life is hunted down and recycled into the Great Chain.

  

## Blue Assay

Song peeled the packaging off the home pregnancy test and stood, staring at the test result window for a long moment, before stepping out into her hallway and heading to the bathroom that she shared with three other apartments on her floor.

Her menstrual cycles had been late now and then. However, there was something going on. The late November weather had been unseasonably warm. Sydney spent several days under a blanket of smoke that caught the world's attention. 

But Song was used to hot weather. Her studies in Japan had spanned two summers, where high humidity accompanied temperatures that often hit 38 degrees C. 

The  heat didn’t normally bother her much at all. Lately, she was feeling hot on her forehead, hot on the back of her legs. The cool of her apartment seemed to make this more pronounced.

She locked the bathroom door behind her and shut her eyes. Her intuition said that the test was unnecessary. She was pregnant.

She felt the need for modern medicine to confirm this before telling Adrian. A dizzying set of forks appeared in her future path. 

Three minutes later

![][image3]

…appeared in the results window. She snapped a picture with her phone.  
The shaggy old bathroom had a single fluorescent tube above a mirrored cabinet and ancient ceramic sink. She leant on the sink with both hands rotated back towards her and stared into her eyes. 

There was no stream of inner monologue. Her eyes looked back at herself. They widened as she imagined looking into her child’s eyes. And the child was looking back at her. She felt a powerful wave of Life and cried true tears of Joy. The fluorescent tube flickered, the hum of its ballast staccato followed the flicker.

The tears were no burden. This was a “Happening”. Conversations from her uncle came to mind. A wild river in flood had been inviting her to fall. To fall in. To fall for Adrian’s laconic charm. The sexual attraction was the scariness of a wild river. She had Dove In weeks ago.

At the moment of the pregnancy test’s result, she realised she was already in the River. The idea that she was looking into her child’s eyes at this moment gave her a key insight into life. This was no time to invite fear into this situation. For the sake of the child that formed in her belly, she put aside fear.

“There’s only one way to go, and that’s with The Flow” she said outside. The observant universe both inspired and agreed with that statement.

The tears were still flowing, but they caused her no heavy sensations. She washed her face and hands and headed out of the bathroom to dress. Adrian might be in for a shock. 

She felt compelled to find him and tell him now.

## Red and Black

Adrian was returning to the table of his friends and colleagues with a round of drinks.  He smiled, watching Sparkles deep in conversation with Roy and Vickie. His phone buzzed insistently in his breast pocket. 

He set down the tray of beers. A message from Greg. And three missed calls from Song. He excused himself and walked off to call her back.

May and Eric were shuffling another table into place with the group. Eric shook Heenie’s hand and introduced himself. “That’s one hell of a handshake Heenie. No limp dead fish there\!”

“Sparkles.” she said. “Friends call me Sparkles, Sparky \- variations on that.” She introduced herself to Mae and described her handshake method. “The handshake is mostly from my Aunty Tia. She was all about ‘straight down the middle. Match the other person’s grip. No twisting or being twisted by the hand-shaker’”. The table reacted with positive comments and nods and laughter. 

“Sam is gonna love that.” said Eric. Heenie looked at him, waiting for a backstory. “Oh, we have two senior gentlemen working with us \- colloquially known as Ralph and Sam. It’s actually great having them around. They are old school, but… new cool.” Laughter. Heenie acknowledged the Warner Brothers cartoon reference.

Eric continued. “Ernest Bennington, that’s Greg’s uncle. He’s kind of a stoic mathematical genius wrapped in an old buffalo hide that’s become a part of his skin.” More laughter.

“I know he loves a strong handshake. He loves other reminders of what might have been.. a more honourable time.” Eric smiled in his conclusion.

Heeni lifted her glass. “Cheers everyone. I feel like I landed in the right place tonight.” Glasses clinked around the table. Adrian was returning, talking to Greg as he arrived. 

The conversation flowed and glowed as Dixon street’s restaurants hung their Specials boards and opened their doors.

Song arrived from the Haymarket direction. She was somewhat taken aback by the energetics of her tables of friends. Looks like Dr Cooper had been at work for a while.

She hesitated before making her arrival known. Her news might need to wait a while. 

Song bought herself a strategic tonic water and joined the buzzing tables.

— 

The conversation wound around world events and Everyone’s day. Heenie had been passing off questions about her sudden arrival as a need to see the “bright lights” cousin Adrian had talked about. With a few standard drinks in her bloodstream, her trust of the group rose as her inhibitions fell away.

She described her arrival at home and the signals that her Dad was ‘with’ someone new.

“It’s barely been a year.” she said. “I don’t want Chris to live his whole life dead from the neck down \- but, I’m not ready for a Step.. girlfriend.” Laughter.

Vickie held up her glass to the Y chromosomes around the table. “You guys”. She shook her head. “You get some blood flow into your dick and your emotional role in other relationships just flatlines.”

Mae quoted a variation of her grandmother’s generalisation. “Patience is a virtue. Get it if you can. Seldom found in women and Never in a Man\!”

Greg spoke up. “Thanks for the vote of confidence Grandma\!”

“Well, it sucks all the same Heeni”. It was Song, who’d been a little detached from the conversation so far. “I can imagine it was a real kick in the stomach for you Sparkles. But, let’s not be having any of that ‘don’t run away’ bullshit\!” This drew agreement and encouragement around the table.

Heeni looked at Song with gratitude in her eyes. “Thanks Song. Thanks to all of you guys. My Aunty was supportive too.” She pulled a roll of $100 notes out of her jeans pocket. “Hell, she’s funding this little adventure\!” This drew a great reaction from the table. “I guess it’s my round,” Heenie said, standing.   
Adrian spoke up. “You got an ID cuzz?”

Song over-rode the moment. “Come on Sparkles. I’ll become an active conspirator in this round.” 

They walked into the bar together, leaving wistful humour to murmur its way around the Gang.

## Three Wolves

Ern washed his face in the bathroom and returned to his desk. A message from Bob \- he wanted to talk. 

“It’s a little late for you Ern?” Pak Lim had arrived while Ern was in the bathroom. They normally had their handover chat at dusk. They were both late today.

“So are you my friend. Been to the gym, I see.” Ern observed,  eyeing off the radiance of Pak’s forehead. “That's an off pattern for you Pak.”

“Yes, it is.” Pak replied, taking a seat beside Ern. “I had a disturbed sleep. Some dreams. Dreams about wolves. I felt very vague when I tried to get up and move for work. So I did a gym class, a boxing class. I’m awake now.”

“Pak, my friend. I don’t mean to be rude, but I have a conversation with an old friend that will start soon. We normally have very private conversations.”

Pak nodded. “Of course, I understand. I’ll work in the conference room”. He stood to move away.

“Dreams about Wolves. That’s a powerful memory.” said Ern. “They’re a powerful symbol in dreams. Was it a positive dream?”

“Hard to say,” replied Pak. “I was in a forest, a fresh fall of snow going on. I was hiking up a hill as the sun set. Without any sounds, I knew I was being watched from behind. I turned and faced an elderly grey wolf, 200 metres behind me.”

“I was on a narrowing hillside, heading towards a peak. There were two other wolves, full grown but very young. The three wolves were triangulating me towards the invisible peak.”

“I ran. The memory of fear sweeping over me in my dream is very strong. As I reached the peak, they were nearly upon me.”

Pak paused in his story, stormy weather behind his eyes.

Ern did his best to be patient. Soon, that expired. “So, what happened?”  
Pak resumed. “The top of the peak was like a cliff, hundreds of metres above a river valley. I could barely stop in time to avoid the fall. The wolves were ready to pounce. The two younger wolves hung back, leaving me for the Alpha.”

Pak was quiet again. Then he spoke. “I fell backwards as the Alpha attacked. I grabbed at his jowls  to fend him off.”

“I rolled backwards, using the Alpha’s momentum. I threw him off the cliff.” Pak concluded.

Ern stared at Pak’s intensity that came with the dream's climax. 

“The younger wolves ran.” Pak concluded.

Ern’s computer was chiming. He typed briefly, asking Bob to hold five.

Ern smiled. “Remind me not to piss you off, Pak.”

Pak returned the smile. “You are more of a Father Bear my friend. I’ll leave you to talk to your comrade.”

Final Thoughts

You are a little wisp of soul carrying a corpse.

Identity Protective Cognition

Acting

Persona and persona system services

Thoughts as reflection of sentences and other thoughts

Idem \- same, oneness.

Where IT was EYE shall become

Simplicity

Senses

Story

The Daemon.

Death and Rebirth

Departure and return.

In defining what *isn’t,* you can focus on what *is*. Put another way, by clarifying where you *won’t be*, you can put energy into the places you *are*.

Heeni splits into two selves. Uncle Ern. Her 17 year old self. She also dies and watches the birth of the universe from a cosmic egg. Ern becomes aware of what has happened and let’s his life pass so that 17 year old Heeni can live. Heeni falls in love with Song. 

Smell is the ultimate Illusion sense.

Song meets Adrian at her gallery opening. They have a love affair. Birth control doesn’t work, Song becomes pregnant. Adrian spins out and tries a runner. The pandemic arrives \- his flight to NZ is cancelled.  
Pak-Lim reads the Secrets with Symmetry and decides to split test the protocol with their SSID product. 

Eric does the math. Writes up a proof and sends it to @man via Mae. 

The sws webservice comes online. It produces a digital image with a self-referential key stored in the image metadata. 

Pairing to Peering

The concept of individual identity being what drops during enlightenment becomes the main theme.

Identity feels like gravity.

Universal Identity doesn’t need to ‘pull us in’ because we are already there. The universal identity is the gravity

It’s the tension around individual identity and the universal Identity that is the drama that drives Life forwards. No better example than language.

It’s a metaphor on metaphor system, pointing to root meaning, because that’s what metaphors do. All of this is in aid of craving and aversion.

Time’s passage works like metaphors in language. Pointing at ‘something else’, the future, the past.
